Find the perimeter of the shape at the nth step of the sequence. Take the starting equilateral triangle to have a sidelength of 1 unit.

Solution

Let {tex}{Pn}={/tex} perimeter at step n
Step 0:

  • Side length {tex}=1{/tex} unit
  • Number of sides {tex}=3{/tex}
  • {tex}{P}_0=3 \times 1=3{/tex} units

Step 1:

  • Each side of length 1 is divided into 3 parts, each of length {tex}1 / 3{/tex}
  • Each side becomes 4 segments of length {tex}1 / 3{/tex}
  • Number of sides {tex}=12{/tex}
  • {tex}P_1=12 \times(1 / 3)=4{/tex} units

Step 2:

  • Each side of length {tex}1 / 3{/tex} becomes 4 segments of length {tex}1 / 9{/tex}
  • Number of sides {tex}=48{/tex}
  • {tex}{P}_2=48 \times(1 / 9)=16 / 3{/tex} units

Pattern:

  • At step {tex}n{/tex}, each original side is divided into {tex}3^n{/tex} equal parts
  • Length of each small segment {tex}=1 / 3^n{/tex}
  • Number of sides {tex}=3 \times 4^n{/tex}
  • Perimeter {tex}{Pn}=\left(3 \times 4^n\right) \times\left(1 / 3^n\right){/tex}
  • Therefore, {tex}{P n}={3} \boldsymbol{\times}({4} / {3})^{{n}}{/tex} units

sections.label: Visualising Solids
sections.html:
Q 1

Picture your name, then read off the letters backwards. Make sure to do this by sight, not by sound - really see your name! Now try with your friend's name.

Solution

This is a visualization exercise. Let's take an example:
If your name is "RAVI":

  • Visualize: R-A-V-I
  • Reading backwards by sight: I-V-A-R

If your friend's name is "PRIYA":

  • Visualize: P-R-I-Y-A
  • Reading backwards by sight: A-Y-I-R-P

Practice this with different names to improve visual memory and spatial thinking.

Q 2

Cut off the four corners of an imaginary square, with each cut going between midpoints of adjacent edges. What shape is left over? How can you reassemble the four corners to make another square?

Solution

Shape left over: When we cut off the four corners by joining midpoints of adjacent edges, we get a regular octagon (8-sided polygon with all sides equal).
Explanation:

  • Each corner cut creates a new edge
  • The original 4 sides of the square remain but become shorter
  • Total edges {tex}=4{/tex} (from original sides) +4 (from corner cuts) {tex}=8{/tex} edges
  • All sides are equal in length

Reassembling the four corners: The four corner pieces are identical right-angled isosceles triangles. To make another square:

  • Arrange the four triangles so that their right angles meet at the center
  • The hypotenuses of the triangles form the sides of a new square
  • This new square has half the area of the original square
  • Alternatively, arrange two triangles to form a square, then place the other two similarly
Q 3

Mark the sides of an equilateral triangle into thirds. Cut off each corner of the triangle, as far as the marks. What shape do you get?

Solution

When we mark the sides of an equilateral triangle into thirds and cut off each corner at these marks, we get a regular hexagon (6-sided polygon with all sides equal).
Explanation:
Original triangle has 3 sides
Cutting each corner creates 2 new edges per corner
From 3 corners, we get {tex}3 \times 2=6{/tex} new edges
The middle third of each original side remains
Total edges {tex}=3{/tex} (middle parts of original sides) +6 (from corner cuts) {tex}=9{/tex} edges initially

Wait, let's reconsider:

  • Each corner is cut, removing the first third and last third meeting point
  • This creates one new edge per corner {tex}=3{/tex} new edges
  • Each original side now contributes its middle third {tex}=3{/tex} edges
  • Total {tex}=3+3={6}{/tex} edges forming a regular hexagon

All six sides are equal because each is one-third of the original side length.

Q 4

Mark the sides of a square into thirds and cut off each of its corners as far as the marks. What shape is left?

Solution

When we mark the sides of a square into thirds and cut off each corner at these marks, we get a regular octagon ( 8 -sided polygon with all sides equal).

Explanation:

  • Original square has 4 sides
  • Cutting each corner creates 1 new edge per corner
  • From 4 corners, we get 4 new edges
  • The middle third of each original side remains {tex}=4{/tex} edges
  • Total edges {tex}=4{/tex} (middle parts) +4 (from corner cuts) {tex}=8{/tex} edges

All eight sides are equal because:

  • The middle third of each original side has the same length
  • Each cut edge (diagonal across the corner) also has the same length by symmetry

This forms a regular octagon.

Q 5

Can you describe a solid and a viewpoint that would result in each of the following cases? If it helps, you can imagine the solid passing through a wall like Tom did, and leaving a hole of the appropriate shape.

  1. A solid whose profile has a square outline
  2. A solid whose profile has a circular outline
  3. A solid whose profile has a triangular outline
Solution
  1. Several solids can have a square profile: Example: A cube viewed directly from the front, back, left, right, top, or bottom will show a square outline.
    1. Cube - viewed from any face directly
    2. Cuboid - viewed from a face that is square
    3. Square prism - viewed from the top or bottom
    4. Square pyramid - viewed from the base (looking down)
  2. Several solids can have a circular profile:Example: A cylinder viewed from directly above or below shows a circular outline.
    1. Sphere - from any viewpoint
    2. Cylinder - viewed from the top or bottom (along the axis)
    3. Cone - viewed from the base (looking at the circular base)
    4. Hemisphere - from the curved side or flat side
  3. Several solids can have a triangular profile:
    1. Triangular pyramid (tetrahedron) - viewed from any face
    2. Triangular prism - viewed from the triangular face
    3. Cone - viewed from the side
    4. Wedge - viewed from the triangular end
    Example: A cone viewed from the side shows a triangular outline (isosceles triangle)
Q 6

As we saw with the elephant, a given solid might have very different profiles from different viewpoints. Can you visualise solids that have the following contrasting profiles?
Spend some time on this, and if you are finding it difficult to visualise, you may look around and use objects that are around you, or that you will make in the next section. Feel free to consider viewpoints from any direction, including directly above the object.

  1. A solid with a rectangular profile from one viewpoint and a circular profile from another viewpoint
  2. A solid with a circular profile from one viewpoint and a triangular one from another viewpoint
  3. A solid with a rectangular profile from one viewpoint and a triangular one from another viewpoint
  4. A solid with a trapezium shaped profile from one viewpoint and a circular one from another viewpoint
  5. A solid with a pentagonal profile from one viewpoint and a rectangular one from another viewpoint
Solution
  1. Cylinder is the perfect example. Another example: Cone (though the side view would be triangular, not rectangular)
    • Rectangular profile: When viewed from the side (perpendicular to the axis), it shows a rectangle
    • Circular profile: When viewed from the top or bottom (along the axis), it shows a circle
      Another example: Cone (though the side view would be triangular, not rectangular)
  2. Cone is the best example.
    • Circular profile: When viewed from the base (looking at the bottom), it shows a circle
    • Triangular profile: When viewed from the side, it shows a triangle (isosceles)
  3. Triangular Prism is a good example.
    • Rectangular profile: When viewed from the side (perpendicular to the triangular face), it shows a rectangle
    • Triangular profile: When viewed from the front or back (facing the triangular face), it shows a triangle
      Another example: Triangular pyramid (tetrahedron) when viewed from different angles.
  4. Truncated Cone (Frustum of a cone) is the perfect example.
    • Trapezium profile: When viewed from the side, it shows a trapezium (the slanting sides with parallel top and bottom)
    • Circular profile: When viewed from the top or bottom, it shows a circle
      This shape is commonly seen in lampshades or buckets.
  5. Pentagonal Prism is the example.
    • Pentagonal profile: When viewed from the front or back (facing the pentagonal face), it shows a pentagon
    • Rectangular profile: When viewed from any of the five sides (perpendicular to the pentagonal face), it shows a rectangle

Note: There are multiple possible solids for each condition, showing that three-dimensional visualization requires considering multiple perspectives.

Q 7

If the congruent polygons of a prism have 10 sides, how many faces, edges and vertices does the prism have? What if the polygons have n sides?

Solution

For a prism with 10 -sided polygons:
Faces:

  • 2 congruent decagonal ( 10 -sided) faces (top and bottom)
  • 10 rectangular faces (connecting the sides)
  • Total faces {tex}=2+10=12{/tex} faces

Edges:

  • 10 edges on the top polygon
  • 10 edges on the bottom polygon
  • 10 vertical edges connecting corresponding vertices
  • Total edges {tex}=10+10+10=30{/tex} edges

Vertices:

  • 10 vertices on the top polygon
  • 10 vertices on the bottom polygon
  • Total vertices {tex}=10+10=20{/tex} vertices

For a prism with {tex}{n}{/tex}-sided polygons:

  • Faces {tex}=2+n=(n+2){/tex} faces
  • Edges {tex}=n+n+n=3 n{/tex} edges
  • Vertices = n + n = 2n vertices
Q 8

If the base of a pyramid has 10 sides, how many faces, edges and vertices does the pyramid have? What if the base is an n-sided polygon?

Solution

For a pyramid with a 10 -sided base:
Faces:

  • 1 decagonal ( 10 -sided) base
  • 10 triangular faces (one for each side of the base)
  • Total faces {tex}=1+10=11{/tex} faces

Edges:

  • 10 edges on the base
  • 10 slant edges from base vertices to apex
  • Total edges {tex}=10+10=20{/tex} edges

Vertices:

  • 10 vertices on the base
  • 1 apex vertex at the top
  • Total vertices {tex}=10+1=11{/tex} vertices

For a pyramid with {tex}{n}{/tex}-sided base:

  • Faces {tex}=1+n=({n}+{1}){/tex} faces
  • Edges {tex}=n+n=2 n{/tex} edges
  • Vertices = n + 1 = ( {tex}{n}{/tex} + 1) vertices
Q 9

What is a net of a regular tetrahedron? Which of the following are nets of a regular tetrahedron? Are there any other possible nets?

Solution

A regular tetrahedron has 4 equilateral triangular faces.
The two possible nets of a regular tetrahedron are:
Net 1: Three triangles in a row with one triangle attached to the middle triangle
Net 2: All four triangles arranged in a connected pattern where they share edges differently
A regular tetrahedron has only 2 possible nets (considering rotations and reflections as the same).
Why only 2?

  • A tetrahedron has 4 faces
  • Each face must share an edge with 3 other faces
  • The limited number of faces and connectivity constraints result in only 2 distinct unfolding patterns
Q 10

Draw a net with appropriate measurements that can be folded into a regular tetrahedron. Verify if it works by making an actual cutout.

Solution

For a regular tetrahedron with edge length 6 cm:
Net design: Draw 4 equilateral triangles, each with side length 6 cm, arranged in one of the two patterns mentioned above.

Steps:

  1. Draw an equilateral triangle with 6 cm sides
  2. Attach three more equilateral triangles to each side of the first triangle
  3. Add small flaps (about 1 cm) on some edges for glueing
  4. Cut out the net
  5. Fold along the edges
  6. Glue the flaps to form the tetrahedron

Verification: When folded correctly, all 4 faces should be equilateral triangles, and all 6 edges should be 6 cm long.

Q 11

Draw a net with appropriate measurements that can be folded into a square pyramid. Verify if it works by making an actual cutout.

Solution

For a square pyramid with:

  • Base edge = 6 cm
  • Slant edge = 5 cm

Net design:

  1. Draw a square with 6 cm sides (the base)
  2. Attach 4 isosceles triangles to each side of the square
  3. Each triangle has:
    • Base = 6 cm (shared with square)
    • Two equal sides = 5 cm each

Steps:

  1. Draw a 6 cm {tex}\times{/tex} 6 cm square in the center
  2. On each side, draw an isosceles triangle with base 6 cm and equal sides 5 cm
  3. Add small flaps for gluing
  4. Cut out the net
  5. Fold the triangles up
  6. Glue the adjacent triangles to form the pyramid

Verification: The base should be square, and the 4 triangular faces should meet at a common apex.

Q 12

What are the sidelengths of the rectangle obtained (from unfolding a cylinder)?

Solution

When a cylinder is unfolded into its net:

The rectangle has:

  • Length = Circumference of the circular base = 2{tex}\pi{/tex}r (where r is the radius of the base)
  • Width (or height) = h (the height of the cylinder)

Explanation:

  • When we "unroll" the curved surface, the distance around the circle becomes the length of the rectangle
  • The height of the cylinder becomes the width of the rectangle
  • The two circular faces remain as circles

Complete net of cylinder consists of:

  • 1 rectangle with dimensions 2{tex}\pi{/tex}r {tex}\times{/tex} h
  • 2 circles with radius r
Q 13

How will the net of a cone look?

Solution

The net of a cone consists of two parts:

  1. Circular base - A complete circle with radius r
  2. Curved surface - A sector of a larger circle with:
    • Radius = l (slant height of the cone)
    • Arc length = 2{tex}\pi{/tex}r (circumference of the base)
    • Central angle θ = (2{tex}\pi{/tex}r)/l × 180°/{tex}\pi{/tex} = (360r)/l degrees

Shape: The curved surface unfolds into a "pac-man" or "pizza slice" shape - a sector of a circle.
Why a sector?

  • All points on the base circle are equidistant from the apex
  • When unrolled, they remain equidistant, forming an arc
  • The slant edges become the radii of the sector
Q 14

What surface do you construct by using the above net, in which O is not the centre of the boundary circle? Make a physical model to help you answer this question!

Solution

If we use a sector of a circle where O (the apex of the original cone) is NOT at the center of the boundary circle, we cannot construct a cone.

What we get instead:

  • If O is inside the boundary circle (but not at center): We get a portion of a cone or an irregular cone-like surface
  • If O is outside the boundary circle: The surface cannot close properly

For a proper cone:

  • O must be at the center of curvature of the arc
  • The arc length must equal the circumference of the base circle
  • The radius of the sector equals the slant height

Experiment: Try making different sectors and see what surfaces form. Only when the geometry is correct (O at center, proper arc length) will you get a proper cone.

Q 15

Can you visualise its net (octahedron)?

Solution

Yes, the net of an octahedron can be visualized.

An octahedron has:

  • 8 equilateral triangular faces
  • 12 edges
  • 6 vertices

Net pattern: The net shown in the textbook displays 8 triangles arranged in a specific pattern.
This looks like a diamond or elongated hexagon shape made of triangles.
How it folds:

  • The triangles fold up and meet at vertices
  • Opposite triangular faces are parallel
  • It creates a 3D shape with 8 faces

Other nets exist: Like the cube, an octahedron has 11 different possible nets.

Q 16

Taking all the triangles in the net to be equilateral, make a cutout of the net and fold it to form an octahedron.

Solution

Steps to make an octahedron:

  1. Draw the net: Draw 8 equilateral triangles with side length 5 cm arranged in the pattern shown in the textbook
  2. Arrangement: Use the diamond pattern:
    • 1 triangle at top
    • 4 triangles in the middle row
    • 2 triangles in the next row
    • 1 triangle at bottom
  3. Add flaps: Add small tabs (about 0.5 cm) on appropriate edges for gluing
  4. Cut out: Carefully cut along the outer boundary
  5. Score the folds: Use a ruler and blunt object to score the edges between triangles
  6. Fold and glue:
    • Fold all the triangles upward
    • Glue the flaps to adjacent triangles
    • Ensure all 8 faces are equilateral triangles

Result: You should get a 3D octahedron - it looks like two square pyramids joined at their bases.
Note: As mentioned, an octahedron has 11 different nets, just like a cube.

Q 17

What is the shortest path for the ant to reach the laddu (when laddu is at the centre of the top face and ant is at the centre of the side face)?

Solution

To find the shortest path on the surface of a cuboid, we use the net of the cuboid.
Method:

  1. Draw the net of the cube
  2. Mark the position of the ant on the side face
  3. Mark the position of the laddu on the top face
  4. On the net, these two points lie on a flat surface
  5. Draw a straight line between them on the net
  6. This straight line represents the shortest path

Why this works:

  • On a flat surface, the shortest distance between two points is a straight line
  • When we unfold the cube into a net, the surface becomes flat
  • Any path on the cube transfers to a path of the same length on the net
  • Therefore, the shortest path on the net (straight line) corresponds to the shortest path on the cube

Visual result: The path will go diagonally across the side face and continue onto the top face, reaching the laddu.

Q 18

What about in the following case (when laddu is at the centre of the edge)?

Solution

When the laddu is at the center of an edge, we again use the net method:

Analysis:

  1. Unfold the cube into a net
  2. Mark the ant's position (center of side face)
  3. Mark the laddu's position (center of an edge)
  4. Draw a straight line between them on the net

Important consideration:

  • The cube can be unfolded in different ways
  • The shortest path depends on which faces we unfold together
  • We need to check different net configurations
  • Choose the net that gives the shortest straight-line distance

Result: The shortest path will be different from the first case, and the ant might travel across different faces depending on which edge the laddu is on.

Q 19

Are either of these the shortest path?

Solution

To determine if a given path is the shortest:

Test method:

  1. Draw the net of the cuboid
  2. Mark the ant and laddu positions on the net
  3. Draw the proposed path on the net
  4. Check if it is a straight line on the net

Results:

  • If the path is a straight line on the net {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} YES, it is the shortest path
  • If the path is NOT a straight line on the net {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} NO, it is NOT the shortest path

Example from textbook:

  • First path: When drawn on the net, it appears as a straight line {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} YES, shortest path
  • Second path: When drawn on the net, it is NOT a straight line {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} NO, not the shortest path

Key insight: A path that looks curved or indirect on the 3D cube might actually be straight on the net, and vice versa!

Q 20

Find the shortest path between the ant and the laddu in the following case:

Solution

To find the shortest path, we need to unfold the cuboid correctly.

Step 1: Identify the position of ant and laddu

  • Ant is at the center of one face
  • Laddu is at a specific position on the cuboid

Step 2: Unfold the cuboid in different ways
When we unfold the cuboid, we must ensure that the path between ant and laddu stays on the surface.
Important Point: The way a cuboid is unfolded matters! If the line segment goes outside the net, it doesn't correspond to any actual path on the cuboid.
Step 3: Choose correct unfolding
We need to unfold the cuboid such that:

  • The ant and laddu are on the same flat surface
  • We can draw a straight line between them
  • This line represents the actual path on the cuboid's surface


The correct unfolding will give us the shortest straight-line distance between the ant and laddu on the unfolded surface, which corresponds to the shortest path on the actual cuboid.

Q 21

What is the length of the shortest path between the ant and the laddu?

Solution

Given:

  • Dimensions: 30 cm {tex}\times{/tex} 12 cm {tex}\times{/tex} 6 cm
  • Ant position: 1 cm from edge
  • Laddu: stuck to the back of the box, 1 cm from edge

Method: We need to unfold the cuboid in different possible ways and calculate the distance in each case.

Case 1: First unfolding. When we unfold in one way, we get a certain distance.
Case 2: Second unfolding (as shown)

  • The unfolded dimensions create a right triangle
  • One side = 24 cm
  • Other side = 32 cm

Using Baudhayana Theorem (Pythagoras Theorem): {tex}d^2=24^2+32^2 d^2=576+1024{/tex}
{tex} d^2=1600 d=\sqrt{1600 } d=40 c m {/tex}
Important observation: In each unfolding, the lengths of line segments between ant and laddu are different!
Solution: We must carefully list all possible different unfoldings and calculate the distance in each case. The minimum of all these distances will be the shortest path.
Therefore, we need to check all possible unfoldings to find the actual shortest path. From the given unfolding, one possible answer is 40 cm, but we should verify if other unfoldings give a shorter distance.

Q 22

What happens to the length of a line in its projection?

Solution

Let {tex}{l}={/tex} actual length of the line
Let {tex}{p}={/tex} length of its projection
From Fig.

  • Draw {tex}{AE} \perp {BC}{/tex}
  • AECD is a rectangle (because opposite sides are parallel and all angles are {tex}90^{\circ}{/tex}
  • Therefore, {tex}{AE}={DC}={p}{/tex}
  • Also, {tex}\angle {AEB}=90^{\circ}{/tex}

Comparison: In right triangle AEB:

  • {tex}{AB}={l}{/tex} (actual length)
  • {tex}{AE}={p}{/tex} (projection length)
  • {tex}\angle {AEB}=90^{\circ}{/tex}

By Baudhayana Theorem: {tex}A B^2=A E^2+E B^2 {/tex}, {tex}l^2=p^2+E B^2{/tex}
Since {tex}{EB}^2 \geq 0{/tex}, we get: {tex}{l}^2 \geq {p}^2{/tex}
Therefore: {tex}{l} \geq {p}{/tex}
The projection length is always less than or equal to the actual length.

Q 23

When is the length of the projected line equal to its actual length?

Solution

The projection length equals the actual length when EB = 0
This happens when:

  • Point E coincides with point B
  • The line AB is parallel to the plane of projection
  • The line lies in a plane parallel to the projection plane

Therefore: The projected line length equals actual length when the line is parallel to the plane of projection.

Q 24

What do you think are the different possible projections of a square that we get based on its orientation?

Solution

Based on different orientations, a square can have the following projections:

  1. Square: When the square is parallel to the projection plane
  2. Rectangle: When the square is tilted at an angle to the projection plane
  3. Line segment: When the square is perpendicular to the projection plane (edge-on view)
  4. Parallelogram: Generally not possible for a square, as opposite sides remain parallel and equal in projection
Q 25

What do you think is the projection of a parallelogram under different orientations? Can this ever be a quadrilateral that is not a parallelogram?

Solution

Projection of a parallelogram:
Under different orientations, a parallelogram can project as:

  1. Parallelogram: Most common case
  2. Rectangle: Special case when oriented appropriately
  3. Line segment: When perpendicular to projection plane
  4. Smaller parallelogram: When tilted

Can it be a non-parallelogram quadrilateral?
No! The projection of a parallelogram is always a parallelogram (or a degenerate case like a line segment).
Reason:

  • Parallel lines always project to parallel lines.
  • Since opposite sides of a parallelogram are parallel, their projections will also be parallel.
  • A quadrilateral with both pairs of opposite sides parallel is a parallelogram.
Q 26

What can you say about the projection of an n-sided regular polygon?

Solution

For an n-sided regular polygon:
When parallel to projection plane:

  • Projection is the same n-sided regular polygon
  • All sides and angles are preserved

When tilted:

  • Projection is an n-sided polygon (same number of sides)
  • It may not be regular anymore
  • Opposite sides that were parallel remain parallel in projection
  • The shape becomes a compressed or stretched version

Special cases:

  • When perpendicular: Projects to a line segment
  • When at intermediate angles: Projects to an n-sided polygon with modified angles and side lengths

Q 27

Find another object that makes the same projection as that of a given cone.

Solution

Several objects can make the same projection as a cone:

  1. Cylinder: When viewed from the side, gives the same rectangular projection as a cone
  2. Truncated cone (Frustum): Can give similar projections from certain angles
  3. Another cone of different height: A cone with different dimensions but same base diameter can give the same circular top view
  4. Hemisphere: The top view (projection on horizontal plane) of both cone and hemisphere is a circle

Examples based on the three views:

  • Top View (Circle): Cone, Cylinder, Sphere, Hemisphere all give circular top view
  • Front View (Triangle): Cone, Pyramid with triangular/square base can give triangular front view
  • Side View: Similar to front view for symmetrical objects
Q 28

Construct a model of a cube and use your hands to keep it balanced on one corner vertex. Can you try to understand why all the projected edges have equal length?

Solution

Experiment:

  1. Take a cube and balance it on one vertex
  2. The main diagonal (from this vertex to opposite vertex) is now vertical
  3. Project it down to a horizontal plane


Why all edges have equal length in projection:
Symmetry Analysis:
When a cube is balanced on one vertex:

  • The main diagonal is perpendicular to the projection plane
  • All 12 edges of the cube make the same angle with the projection plane
  • Due to this symmetry, all edges project to equal lengths


Mathematical Reasoning:
From earlier, we learned:

  • Projection length = Actual length {tex}\times{/tex} cos({tex}\theta{/tex})
  • Where {tex}\theta{/tex} is the angle between the edge and the projection plane

For a cube balanced on vertex:

  • All edges make the same angle {tex}\theta{/tex} with the horizontal plane
  • Therefore, all projections have same length = edge length × cos({tex}\theta{/tex})

Geometric Visualization:
The cube has 4-fold rotational symmetry about its main diagonal:

  • Rotating by 90o about the diagonal brings one edge to position of another
  • This symmetry is preserved in projection
  • Hence all edge projections are equal

Result: The projection forms a regular hexagon where all sides are equal because all 12 edges of the cube are equally inclined to the projection plane.
This is the principle behind isometric projection!

Q 29

Why is this correspondence between directions on isometric paper and axes of the solid so effective for communicating the shape of the solid?

Solution

The isometric correspondence is effective for several reasons:

  1. Parallel Lines Remain Parallel:
    • In the solid: edges parallel to length axis are parallel to each other
    • In projection: these edges also appear parallel (in direction)
    • This preserves the geometric structure
  2. Equal Scaling Along All Axes:
    • All three axes (length, depth, height) have the same scale
    • If actual edge = 1 unit, its projection = k units (same k for all axes)
    • This makes measurement easy and consistent
  3. Visual Clarity:
    • Our brain can easily interpret three families of parallel lines as three dimensions
    • The three directions (|, , ) are visually distinct
    • Easy to distinguish which edges belong to which axis
  4. Measurement Convenience:
    • Can measure actual dimensions directly from the drawing
    • All three axes use the same grid spacing
    • No complex calculations needed
  5. Preserves Spatial Relationships:
    • If two edges are perpendicular in the solid, their projections make consistent angles
    • Relative positions are maintained
    • Easy to visualize the 3D structure from 2D drawing
  6. Engineering Applications:
    • Standard format recognized worldwide
    • Easy to draw with just ruler and grid paper
    • Can quickly sketch complex 3D objects
    • Facilitates communication between designers and builders

Mathematical Basis:The isometric projection ensures that:

  • Equal actual distances along any axis {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} Equal projected distances
  • This "equal measure" property (iso = equal, metric = measure) makes it intuitive

Conclusion: The combination of parallel line preservation, equal scaling, and visual distinctness makes isometric paper the most effective tool for technical drawing.

sections.label: Figure it Out 2
sections.html:
Q 1

Which of the following are the nets of a cube? First, try to answer by visualisation. Then, you may use cutouts and try.

Solution

Let's analyze each net by visualizing how it would fold:

  1. This arrangement can fold into a cube. When you fold it, all six faces will properly connect. YES, this is a net of a cube.
  2. This arrangement has all 6 squares, but when you try to fold it, two faces will overlap, and one position will be empty. NO, this is NOT a net of a cube.
  3. This arrangement can fold properly into a cube with all faces in correct positions.
    YES, this is a net of a cube.
  4. This arrangement looks like it would work. When folded, all faces align correctly.
    YES, this is a net of a cube.
  5. This arrangement has 6 squares in a straight line. When you fold it, opposite faces will overlap. NO, this is NOT a net of a cube.
  6. This arrangement forms a '{tex}T{/tex}' shape. When folded, it creates a cube properly.

YES, this is a net of a cube.
Summary: (i), (iii), (iv), and (vi) are nets of a cube. (ii) and (v) are NOT nets of a cube.
Verification method:

  • A cube has 6 faces
  • When folded, no two faces should overlap
  • Each face should connect to exactly the right neighbors
Q 2

A cube has 11 possible net structures in total. In this count, two nets are considered the same if one can be obtained from the other by a rotation or a flip. For example, the following nets are all considered the same -

Find all the 11 nets of a cube.

Solution

The 11 distinct nets of a cube are classified by their basic patterns:
Type 1: "1-4-1" pattern ({tex}{1}{/tex} square, {tex}{4}{/tex} in a row, {tex}{1}{/tex} square) - 1 net
One square, then four in a row, then one square attached
Type 2: "2-3-1" pattern ({tex}{2}{/tex} squares, {tex}{3}{/tex} in a row, {tex}{1}{/tex} square) - 3 nets
Different positions of where the 2 squares and 1 square attach to the row of 3
Type 3: "2-2-2" pattern (three pairs) - 1 net
A more symmetric arrangement
Type 4: "1-3-2" pattern - 2 nets
Different configurations
Type 5: "3-2-1" variations - 2 nets
Type 6: Other arrangements - 2 nets
[Students should draw all 11 nets. This requires careful systematic exploration by:

  1. Starting with a row of 4 squares and adding 2 more
  2. Trying different attachment positions
  3. Checking that each folds into a cube
  4. Eliminating duplicates from rotation/reflection]

The key is to ensure each configuration:

  • Has exactly 6 squares
  • Can fold into a cube
  • Is not a rotation or reflection of another net already found
Q 3

Draw a net of a cuboid having sidelengths 5 cm, 3 cm, and 1 cm.

Solution

A cuboid has 6 rectangular faces:

  • 2 faces of dimension {tex}5 {~cm} \times 3 {~cm}{/tex} (top and bottom)
  • 2 faces of dimension {tex}5 {~cm} \times 1 {~cm}{/tex} (front and back)
  • 2 faces of dimension {tex}3 {~cm} \times 1 {~cm}{/tex} (left and right sides)

Students should draw the net with proper measurements labeled on each rectangle.

Q 4

Draw a net of a cuboid having sidelengths 6 cm, 3 cm, and 2 cm.

Solution

A cuboid has 6 rectangular faces:

  • 2 faces of dimension {tex}6 {~cm} \times 3 {~cm}{/tex} (top and bottom)
  • 2 faces of dimension {tex}6 {~cm} \times 2 {~cm}{/tex} (front and back)
  • 2 faces of dimension {tex}3 {~cm} \times 2 {~cm}{/tex} (left and right sides)

Students should draw the net with proper measurements labeled.

Q 5

Net of a sphere? Experiment and see if you can make a paper cutout that can perfectly wrap around a ball without leaving any wrinkles, gaps or overlaps.

Solution

It is IMPOSSIBLE to create a perfect flat net of a sphere.
Why?

  1. Curved surface: A sphere has a continuously curved surface in all directions
  2. No flat net exists: Unlike polyhedra (cube, pyramid, prism) which have flat faces, a sphere's surface cannot be "unfolded" into a flat shape without distortion
  3. Mathematical proof: This is related to Gaussian curvature. A sphere has positive curvature everywhere, while a flat plane has zero curvature. You cannot map one to the other without distortion.

What happens when you try?

  • Wrinkles: If you try to flatten a curved surface, it wrinkles
  • Gaps: If you try to avoid wrinkles, gaps appear
  • Stretching/Overlapping: To cover completely, material must stretch or overlap

Real-world example: This is why world maps always distort the Earth's surface - you cannot perfectly represent a sphere on a flat map.
Approximations:

  • We can use multiple pieces (like a soccer ball - made of pentagons and hexagons)
  • We can use stretchy material
  • But never a perfect single flat net

Try it: Take a piece of paper and try to wrap it around a ball - you'll see it's impossible without creating wrinkles or tears!

Q 6

Have we now completely analysed the problem of finding the shortest path between two points on a cuboid?

Solution

Not completely! There are still considerations:
What we have established:

  1. The shortest path on a cuboid corresponds to a straight line on its net
  2. We can use nets to find shortest paths

What remains:

  1. Multiple nets: A cuboid can be unfolded in different ways, creating different nets
  2. Different paths: Each net might give a different straight-line path
  3. Finding the minimum: We need to check multiple nets and find which gives the shortest distance

Complete solution:

  • Unfold the cuboid in all possible relevant ways
  • For each net, draw a straight line from ant to laddu
  • Calculate the length of each line
  • The shortest among these is the true shortest path

Additional complexity:

  • Some paths might cross edges in ways that aren't immediately obvious
  • We need to ensure the path stays on the surface
  • For complex shapes, this becomes a challenging optimization problem

sections.label: Figure it Out 3
sections.html:
Q 1

Observe the front view, top view and side view of the different lines in Fig. 4.6. Is there any relation between their lengths?

Solution

Yes, there is a definite relation between the lengths of different views.
For a line in 3D space:
Let the actual length of line {tex}=1{/tex}
Observations:

  1. At least one of the three views will show the maximum length
  2. If a line is parallel to one plane, its projection on that plane equals its actual length
  3. Projections on perpendicular planes follow the relation:

Relationship If:

  • Front view length {tex}={I}_1{/tex}
  • Top view length {tex}={I}_2{/tex}
  • Side view length {tex}={I}_3{/tex}
  • Actual length = I

Then: {tex}{I}^{{2}} \boldsymbol{\geq} {I}_{{1}}^{{2}}{/tex} and {tex}{I}^{{2}} \boldsymbol{\geq} {I}_{{2}}^{{2}}{/tex} and {tex}{I}^{{2}} \boldsymbol{\geq} {I}_{{3}}^{{2}}{/tex}
In fact, for certain orientations: {tex}I^2=I_1^2+I_2^2{/tex} (when line is parallel to the side plane)
Conclusion: The actual length is always greater than or equal to any of its projections. The projection lengths are related through the orientation of the line in 3D space.

Q 2

Find the front view, top view and side view of each of the following solids, fixing its orientation with respect to the vertical, horizontal and side planes: cube, cuboid, parallelepiped, cylinder, cone, prism, and pyramid. If needed, see the next problem for clues.

Solution

  1. Cube (assuming standard orientation with faces parallel to planes)

    View Shape Dimensions
    Front View Square {tex}{a} \times {a}{/tex}
    Top View Square {tex}{a} \times {a}{/tex}
    Side View Square {tex}{a} \times {a}{/tex}

  2. Cuboid (dimensions: length l, breadth b, height h)

    View Shape Dimensions
    Front View Rectangle {tex}{l} \times {h}{/tex}
    Top View Rectangle {tex}{l} \times {b}{/tex}
    Side View Rectangle {tex}{b} \times {h}{/tex}

  3. Parallelepiped (all faces are parallelograms)

    View Shape
    Front View Parallelogram
    Top View Parallelogram
    Side View Parallelogram

  4. Cylinder (axis vertical)

    View Shape
    Front View Rectangle
    Top View Circle
    Side View Rectangle

  5. Cone (axis vertical, base on horizontal plane)

    View Shape
    Front View Isosceles Triangle
    Top View Circle
    Side View Isosceles Triangle

  6. Prism (regular prism with square base, axis vertical)

    View Shape
    Front View Rectangle
    Top View Square
    Side View Rectangle
  7. Pyramid (square pyramid, axis vertical)
    View Shape
    Front View Isosceles Triangle
    Top View Square
    Side View Isosceles Triangle
Q 3

Match each of the following objects with its projections.

Solution
  1. Cone (with axis vertical):
    1. Front: Triangle (isosceles)
    2. Top: Circle
    3. Side: Triangle (isosceles)
  2. Cylinder (axis vertical):
    1. Front: Rectangle
    2. Top: Circle
    3. Side: Rectangle
  3. Cuboid:
    1. Front: Rectangle
    2. Top: Rectangle (different dimensions)
    3. Side: Rectangle (different dimensions)
  4. Hexagonal Prism (axis vertical):
    1. Front: Rectangle
    2. Top: Hexagon (regular)
    3. Side: Rectangle
  5. Cube:
    1. Front: Square
    2. Top: Square
    3. Side: Square
  6. Square Pyramid:
    1. Front: Triangle
    2. Top: Square
    3. Side: Triangle

Matching Process:

  1. Look at the top view first - it often gives the best clue
  2. If top is a circle, object is cone, cylinder, or sphere
  3. If top is a polygon, count sides to identify the base
  4. Use front and side views to confirm

Final Matching: Match according to the specific diagrams provided in the actual question.

sections.label: Figure it Out 4
sections.html:
Q 1

Draw the top view, front view and the side view of each of the following combinations of identical cubes.

Solution

To solve this, we need to imagine viewing the stacked cubes from three directions:

  1. First arrangement:
    Front view: Count cubes visible from front:
    • Bottom row: all cubes in front row
    • Upper rows: cubes visible above
    • Draw as a 2D representation
    Top view: Look from directly above:
    • Shows the footprint/base layout
    • Each square represents a column of cubes
    Side view: Look from the side (perpendicular to front):
    Shows height profile from that angle
    General Process:
    • For Front View: Count maximum cubes in each column when viewed from front
    • For Top View: Show the base layout (which positions have cubes)
    • For Side View: Count maximum cubes in each row when viewed from side
  2. Second arrangement:[Follow same process]
  3. Third arrangement:[Follow same process]
  4. Fourth arrangement:[Follow same process]
  5. Fifth arrangement:[Follow same process]
    Important Tips:
    • Front is clearly marked in diagrams
    • Top view shows x-y plane layout
    • Side view is perpendicular to front view
    • Count carefully for stacked cubes
Q 2

Imagine eight identical cubes, glued together along faces to form the letter．

  1. This looks like a from the front. What does it look like from the side? From the top？
  2. Glue additional cubes to make a shape that looks like  from the front and from the top．
  3. Now, can you glue even more cubes to make it look like from the front,  from the top, and from the side？
  4. Can you think of other letter combinations to make with a single combination of cubes in this manner？
Solution
  1. Letter 'E' structure (8 cubes):

    From the front: Letter 'E' (as given) From the side (right side): Looking perpendicular to the front: From the top: Looking down: Top view pattern:
    • 3 cubes in top row
    • 1 cube in middle left
    • 3 cubes in bottom row (if standard E)
    • Total vertical height: 3 units
    • We see the depth profile
    • Appears as: 3 separate horizontal segments at different heights
    • Looks like three separate short rectangles (or squares if single depth)
    • Shows three separate rows
    • Top row: 3 cubes in a line
    • Middle row: 1 cube (left position)
    • Bottom row: 3 cubes in a line
  2. Requirements: Solution:
    Start with 'E' from front (8 cubes minimum)
    For top view to show 'L': Construction:Add cubes strategically: Example arrangement:
    Front view: E
    Top view: L (one vertical line, one horizontal line at bottom) 
    Specific position: Add cubes at positions that maintain both views simultaneously.
    • Front view: 'E'
    • Top view: 'L'
    • Need vertical line (3 cubes)
    • Need horizontal line at bottom (3 cubes)
    • The 'L' should be visible from above
    • Keep front 'E' structure
    • Arrange cubes so from top they form 'L'
    • May need 9-12 cubes total
  3. Requirements: Analysis:
    Letter 'F' from side means:

    (Horizontal line at top, vertical line down with middle horizontal)
    Solution strategy: Construction:
    Position cubes in 3D space such that: This requires careful spatial planning and possibly some cubes serving multiple purposes in different views.
    Yes, it is possible! With approximately 12-18 cubes, we can create this multi-view structure.
    • Front view: 'E'
    • Top view: 'L'
    • Side view: 'F'
    1. Start with structure from part (ii) giving 'E' and 'L'
    2. Add cubes to create 'F' from side without destroying other views
    3. Total cubes needed: approximately 12-15
    • Looking from front {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} 'E' pattern
    • Looking from top {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} 'L' pattern
    • Looking from side {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} 'F' pattern
  4. Yes! Many letter combinations are possible:
    Easier combinations:
    1. I, I, I - A straight vertical column looks like 'I' from all three views
    2. T, T, + - T-shape from two sides, plus from top
    3. L, I, L - L from front, vertical line from top, L from side
    4. C, U, C - Curved shapes
    Moderate combinations:5. E, F, L - As solved above 6. H, I, H - Using 7 cubes 7. T, L, T - T from front, L from top, T from side 8. U, C, U - U shapes from different angles
    Challenging combinations:9. F, L, E - Reverse of (iii) 10. P, L, F - Using specific arrangements
    Strategy for creating:
    • Start with simpler letters (I, L, T, C, U)
    • Use symmetry when possible
    • Each cube can contribute to multiple views
    • Typically need 6-15 cubes for most combinations
    Creative exploration: Try creating your name initials or other meaningful letter combinations!
Q 3

Which solid corresponds to the given top view, front view, and side view?

Solution

To identify the solid from three views:

Process:

  1. Analyze the top view - gives base shape
  2. Analyze the front view - gives height profile
  3. Analyze the side view - confirms structure
  4. Match with given options

For each option:

  1. First solid:
    • Compare top, front, side views
    • Count cubes in each direction
    • Match the pattern
  2. Second solid:
    • Check if views align
    • Verify heights and positions
  3. Third solid:
    • Examine structure
    • Confirm all three views match
  4. Fourth solid:
    Validate against projections
  5. Fifth solid:
    Check consistency
  6. Sixth solid:
    Verify match
  7. Seventh solid:
    Final validation

Answer Method:For each given set of views (Front, Top, Side):

  1. The top view shows which positions have cubes when looking down
  2. The front view shows maximum height at each front-back position
  3. The side view shows maximum height at each left-right position
  4. Reconstruct the 3D structure mentally
  5. Match with given solid options

Matching: [Provide specific matches based on actual diagrams: e.g., Views in question match solid (iii)]

Q 4

Using identical cubes, make a solid that gives the following projections.

Solution

For each set of projections, we reconstruct the 3D solid:

(i) First set of projections:

Given:

  • Top View: Shows base layout
  • Front View: Shows front profile
  • Side View: Shows side profile

Solution:

  1. Start with top view - this shows the footprint (which columns have cubes)
  2. Use front view to determine height of each column in front-back direction
  3. Use side view to verify and confirm heights
  4. Build the solid cube by cube

Example: If top view shows 2{tex}\times{/tex}2 grid, front view shows heights [2,1], side view shows heights [2,1]:

  • Position (1,1): 2 cubes high
  • Position (1,2): 1 cube high
  • Position (2,1): 2 cubes high
  • Position (2,2): 1 cube high

(ii) through (ix): Similar process

General Algorithm:

  1. Mark positions on grid based on top view
  2. For each position, determine maximum height using front and side views
  3. The actual height at position (i,j) is minimum of:
    • Height shown in front view for column i
    • Height shown in side view for row j
  4. Place cubes accordingly

Important Notes:

  • Some configurations may have multiple solutions
  • Ensure all three views match your construction
  • Verify by checking each view of your final solid
Q 5

Find the number of cubes in this stack of identical cubes.

Solution

Method 1: Direct Counting. 
Count visible cubes and estimate hidden ones:

  • Count all visible cubes from front: ________ cubes
  • Count partially hidden cubes: ________ cubes
  • Count completely hidden cubes: ________ cubes

Method 2: Layer by Layer
If we can identify layers:

  • Bottom layer: ________ cubes
  • Second layer: ________ cubes
  • Third layer: ________ cubes
  • Top layer: ________ cubes

Total = Sum of all layers
Method 3: Using Projections

  1. From top view: count positions
  2. From front view: find heights
  3. From side view: verify structure
  4. Calculate total

Method 4: Formula (if regular pattern)If it's a rectangular arrangement of dimensions l {tex}\times{/tex} b {tex}\times{/tex} h:
Total cubes = l {tex}\times{/tex} b {tex}\times{/tex} h
Note: Without the specific diagram, I cannot give the exact number. The answer would be calculated using one of the above methods based on the actual stack shown.
Example: If the stack is 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 3 cube
Total = 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 3 = 27 cubes

Q 6

What are the different shapes the projection of a cube can make under different orientations?

Solution

The projection of a cube can make several different shapes depending on its orientation:

  1. Square
    • When any face is parallel to the projection plane
    • Most common projection
    • All sides equal
  2. Rectangle
    • When cube is tilted slightly
    • Length > Breadth
    • Still has right angles
  3. Parallelogram
    • When cube is tilted more significantly
    • Opposite sides parallel and equal
    • Angles not 90°
  4. Regular Hexagon
    • When cube is balanced on one vertex
    • This is the isometric projection
    • All six sides equal
    • Occurs when cube is oriented such that main diagonal is perpendicular to plane
  5. Non-regular Hexagon
    • At certain intermediate orientations
    • Six sides but not all equal
  6. Line segment
    • Degenerate case
    • When viewing exactly along one edge
    • Very unlikely in practice

Most Important Projections:
Square: Standard view (face parallel to plane)
Rectangle: Slightly tilted
Regular Hexagon: Isometric view (balanced on vertex)
Parallelogram: General tilted view

sections.label: Figure it Out 5
sections.html:
Q 1

In addition to the 5 ways shown in Fig. 4.8, are there any additional ways of gluing four cubes together along faces? Can you visualise and draw these as well?

Solution

The 5 Tetris shapes shown in Fig. 4.8 are:

  1. Straight line (I-shape): 4 cubes in a row
  2. L-shape: 3 cubes in a row + 1 perpendicular
  3. T-shape: 3 cubes in a row + 1 on top of middle
  4. Z-shape (S-shape): Two pairs offset
  5. Square (O-shape): 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 2 arrangement

Additional ways to glue 4 cubes:
Actually, these are the only 5 ways to arrange 4 cubes face-to-face in 2D (called Tetrominos).
However, in 3D, we can have additional arrangements:

6. 3D L-shape:

  • 3 cubes in one plane (L-shape)
  • 1 cube attached perpendicular to the plane
  • Different from 2D L-shape

7. 3D T-shape:

  • 3 cubes in a row
  • 1 cube attached perpendicular (not in same plane as T)

Total possibilities:

  • In 2D (planar): 5 tetrominos (as shown)
  • In 3D (allowing depth): 7-8 different arrangements

Drawing on Isometric Grid:
For each arrangement:

  1. Choose orientation (along which axis)
  2. Draw systematically, cube by cube
  3. Add shading for clarity

Examples:

  1. Straight line along depth: [Draw 4 cubes in a row along direction]
  2. L-shape along length-height: [Draw L in the length-height plane]
  3. T-shape along depth-length with height: [Draw T configuration]
  4. Z-shape: [Draw offset configuration]
  5. Square 2{tex}\times{/tex}2: [Draw 2{tex}\times{/tex}2 arrangement]
Q 2

Draw the following figures on the isometric grid.

Solution

General Process for Drawing on Isometric Grid:
Step 1: Identify the 3 primary axes

  • Height: | (vertical)
  • Depth: (upper left diagonal)
  • Length: (upper right diagonal)

Step 2: Choose a starting point

  • Usually start from the frontmost, lowest, leftmost corner

Step 3: Draw edge by edge. For each edge:

  • Determine its direction (height/depth/length)
  • Determine if it goes in positive or negative direction
  • Count units along that axis
  • Draw accordingly

Step 4: Add shading (optional)

  • Shade different faces with different patterns
  • Helps visualize the 3D structure

Specific Drawings:

  1. First figure:
    1. Analyze the structure
    2. Identify base shape and height
    3. Draw base on isometric grid using and directions
    4. Add vertical edges using | direction
    5. Complete top face
    6. Add shading
  2. Second figure:[Follow same process]
  3. Third figure:[Follow same process]

Tips:

  • Draw lightly first, then darken visible edges
  • Hidden edges can be drawn with dotted lines
  • Use the hint: track whether going up (|) or down (opposite to |)
  • Count grid units carefully

Example Process for an L-shaped figure:

  1. Draw base: 3 units , 1 unit
  2. Draw vertical edges: 2 units |
  3. Draw top horizontal: 3 units  (but at height)
  4. Complete remaining edges
  5. Add shading to distinguish faces

Actual drawings would be provided on isometric grid paper based on specific figures in the textbook.

Q 3

Is there anything strange about the path of this ball? Recreate it on the isometric grid.

Solution

Observation:
Yes, there is something strange! The ball appears to be going in a path that seems to defy gravity or creates an optical illusion.

The strangeness:

  • The ball appears to be rolling "uphill" in a continuous loop, OR
  • The path seems impossible in real 3D space, OR
  • The starting and ending points don't match physically

Why this happens:
This is an impossible figure or optical illusion:

  • It looks correct in 2D (isometric projection)
  • But cannot exist in real 3D space
  • Similar to Escher's famous impossible staircases

Analysis using the hint:
Step 1: Identify physically realizable portion

  • Some parts of the path are normal
  • At some point, there's an impossible connection

Step 2: Identify 3 primary directions

  • Height: |
  • Depth:
  • Length:

Step 3: Recreate on isometric grid
Start from a physical portion:

  1. Draw the track segment that goes in direction
  2. Add the segment going in | direction
  3. Add the segment going in direction
  4. Continue...
  5. At some point, try to connect back - this reveals the impossibility!

The Trick:
The isometric drawing cleverly makes edges that should be at different heights appear to connect. Our brain interprets it as a continuous path, but in reality:

  • Edge A is at height h1
  • Edge B is at height h2 {tex}\neq{/tex} h1
  • But the drawing makes them look connected!

Conclusion: The path is an optical illusion. It looks continuous in the isometric projection but would be discontinuous in real 3D space. This demonstrates that isometric projections can create impossible figures!
Recreation: [Draw the ball path on isometric grid, showing where the impossible connection occurs]

sections.label: The Balancing Act
sections.html:
Q 1

Consider any 2 numbers. Find their average/arithmetic mean. Repeat this by taking other pairs. What do you observe?
For example, let the two numbers be 3 and 7. Their average is {tex}\frac{3+7}{2}=5{/tex}. Taking another pair of numbers, say 8 and 9, their average is {tex}\frac{8+9}{2}=8.5{/tex}. Visualising these as dot plots we get

Solution

Let's take different pairs of numbers and find their averages:
Pair 1: 3 and 7 Average {tex}=(3+7) \div 2=10 \div 2=5{/tex}
Pair 2: 8 and 9 Average {tex}=(8+9) \div 2=17 \div 2=8.5{/tex}
Pair 3: 10 and 20 Average {tex}=(10+20) \div 2=30 \div 2=15{/tex}
Pair 4:5 and 15 Average {tex}=(5+15) \div 2=20 \div 2=10{/tex}
Observation: The mean (average) is always exactly halfway between the two numbers. The distance from the smaller number to the mean equals the distance from the mean to the larger number.

Q 2

Calculate and mark the mean of each collection of data below.

Solution
  1. Collection: 5, 7,9
    {tex} \text { Mean }=(5+7+9) \div 3=21 \div 3=7 {/tex}
    The mean is 7.
  2. Collection: 2, 4, 6, 8
    {tex} \text { Mean }=(2+4+6+8) \div 4=20 \div 4=5 {/tex}
    The mean is 5.
  3. Collection: 10, 10, 11, 17
    {tex} \text { Mean }=(10+10+11+17) {/tex}{tex}\div 4=48 \div 4=12 {/tex}
    The mean is 12.
Q 3

Can you explain how the mean is the centre of each collection?

Solution

The mean is the centre because the sum of distances of all points to the left of the mean equals the sum of distances of all points to the right of the mean.
For example, in collection {tex}(c): 10,10,11,17{/tex} with mean {tex}=12{/tex}:

  • Distance on left: {tex}(12-10)+(12-10)+{/tex}{tex}(12-11)=2+2+1=5{/tex}
  • Distance on right: {tex}(17-12)=5{/tex}

Both distances are equal (5 units each), showing the mean balances the data.

Q 4

Mark the mean for the collections below.

Solution
  1. Collection: 3, 5, 7, 9, 11
    {tex} \text { Mean }=(3+5+7+9+11) {/tex}{tex}\div 5=35 \div 5=7 {/tex}
  2. Collection: 1, 3, 5, 7
    {tex} \text { Mean }=(1+3+5+7){/tex}{tex} \div 4=16 \div 4=4 {/tex}
Q 5

Can you explain how the mean is the centre of each collection?

Solution

For collection (a): 3, 5, 7, 9, 11 with mean {tex}=7{/tex}:

  • Distances on left: {tex}(7-3)+(7-5)=4+2=6{/tex}
  • Distances on right: {tex}(9-7)+(11-7)=2+4=6{/tex}

For collection (b): 1,3,5,7 with mean {tex}=4{/tex} :

  • Distances on left: {tex}(4-1)+(4-3)=3+1=4{/tex}
  • Distances on right: {tex}(5-4)+(7-4)=1+3=4{/tex}

In both cases, the total distances on both sides are equal, confirming the mean is the centre.

Q 6

Can you explain how the mean is the centre of each collection?
Is the mean the midpoint of the two endpoints/extremes of the data?

Solution

No, the mean is not always the midpoint of the two extremes.
For example, in the collection 10, 10, 11, 17:

  • Midpoint of extremes {tex}=(10+17) \div 2=27 \div 2=13.5{/tex}
  • Mean = 12

The mean (12) is different from the midpoint of extremes (13.5).
Instead, the mean is the point where the sum of distances on the left equals the sum of distances on the right.

Q 7

Can there be more than one such 'centre'? In other words, is there any other value such that the sum of the distances to the values lower than it and the values higher than it will still be equal?

Solution

No, there cannot be more than one such centre.
Proof: Consider the collection {tex}10,10,11,17{/tex} with mean {tex}=12{/tex}.
If we take any value greater than 12:

  • All distances on the left side will increase
  • All distances on the right side will decrease
  • The balance is broken

If we take any value less than 12:

  • All distances on the left side will decrease
  • All distances on the right side will increase
  • Again, the balance is broken

Therefore, there is only one unique centre (the mean) where the sum of distances on both sides is equal.

Q 8

Will including a new value in the data increase or decrease the mean?
When a new value greater than the mean is included, the mean increases to maintain the balance between the sum of distances on the LHS and RHS, as illustrated below.

Solution

It depends on the value being included:

  1. If the new value is greater than the current mean: The mean will increase.
    • Example: Data: {tex}4,6,8({/tex} mean {tex}=6){/tex}
    • Adding 10: New data: 4, 6, 8, 10
    • New mean {tex}=(4+6+8+10) \div 4=28 \div 4=7{/tex}
    • The mean increased from 6 to 7.
  2. If the new value is less than the current mean: The mean will decrease.
    • Example: Data: {tex}4,6,8({/tex} mean {tex}=6){/tex}
    • Adding 2: New data: 2,4,6,8
    • New mean {tex}=(2+4+6+8) \div 4=20 \div 4=5{/tex}
    • The mean decreased from 6 to 5.
  3. If the new value equals the mean: The mean will remain the same.
Q 9

What happens to the mean if a value equal to the mean is included or removed?

Solution

Including a value equal to the mean: The mean remains unchanged.

  • Example: Data: {tex}3,5,7{/tex} (mean {tex}=5{/tex} )
  • Adding 5: New data: 3,5,5,7
  • New mean {tex}=(3+5+5+7) \div 4=20 \div 4=5{/tex}
  • Mean stays 5.

Removing a value equal to the mean: The mean remains unchanged.

  • Example: Data: {tex}3,5,5,7{/tex} (mean {tex}=5{/tex})
  • Removing 5: New data: 3, 5, 7
  • New mean {tex}=(3+5+7) \div 3=15 \div 3=5{/tex}
  • Mean stays 5.

Fair-share interpretation: When we include or remove a value equal to the mean, we're not changing the "fair share" amount. Each person already has their fair share, so adding or removing someone with exactly the fair share doesn't change what everyone else has.

Q 10

Explore if it is possible to include or remove 2 values such that the mean is unchanged.

Solution

Yes, this is possible if the total of the three values equals 3 times the mean.
Example: Data: 5, 7, 9 (mean {tex}=7{/tex})
To keep mean at 7:

  • We need 3 values that total to {tex}3 \times 7=21{/tex}
  • Include 4,6 (both less than 7) and 11 (greater than 7)
  • Check: {tex}4+6+{1 1}=2 {1}{/tex}
  • New data: 4, 5, 6, 7, 9, 11
  • New mean {tex}=(4+5+6+7+9+11) \div 6{/tex} {tex}=42 \div 6=7{/tex}
Q 11

Try to include 2 values greater than the mean and 1 value less than the mean, so that the mean stays the same.

Solution

Example: Data: {tex}5,7,9({/tex}mean {tex}=7){/tex}
To keep mean at 7:

  • We need 3 values that total to {tex}3 \times 7=21{/tex}
  • Include 8, 10 (both greater than 7) and 3 (less than 7)
  • Check: {tex}8+10+3=21{/tex}
  • New data: 3, 5, 7, 8, 9, 10
  • New mean {tex}=(3+5+7+8+9+10){/tex}{tex} \div 6=42 \div 6=7{/tex}

Relatively Unchanged!

Q 12

Consider the data: 8, 3, 10, 13, 4, 6, 7, 7, 8, 8, 5. Calculate its mean.

Solution

{tex}{ Sum }=8+3+10+{/tex}{tex}13+4+6+7+7+8+8+5=79 {/tex}
Number of values {tex}=11{/tex}
Mean {tex}=79 \div 11=7.18{/tex} (approximately)

Q 13

Now, consider this data with every value increased by {tex}10: 18,13,20,23,14{/tex}, {tex}16,17,17,18,18,15{/tex}. What is its mean? Is there a quicker way to find out?

Solution

Method 1: (Direct calculation): Sum {tex}=18+13+20+23+14+{/tex}{tex}16+17+17+ 18+18+15=189{/tex}
Mean {tex}=189 \div 11=17.18{/tex} (approximately)
Method 2: (Quicker way): Since every value increased by 10, the mean also increases by 10.
New mean= Original mean {tex}+10=7.18+10=17.18{/tex}
This is much quicker!
Observation: The relative position of the mean stays the same. The entire data set shifts up by 10, so the mean also shifts up by 10.

Q 14

Try to explain, using algebra, what the average is when a fixed number, e.g., 2 is subtracted from every value in the collection.

Solution

Let there be {tex}n{/tex} values: {tex}x_1, x_2, x_3, \ldots x_n{/tex}
Original average {tex}=\left(x_1+x_2+x_3+\ldots+x_n\right) \div n=a{/tex}
When 2 is subtracted from every value:
New average {tex}=\left[\left({x}_1-2\right)+\left({x}_2-2\right)+\left({x}_3-2\right)+\ldots+\left({x}_{{n}}-2\right)\right] \div {n}{/tex}
{tex} =\left[x_1+x_2+x_3+\ldots+x_n-2 n\right] \div n {/tex}
{tex} =\left[\left(x_1+x_2+x_3+\ldots+x_n\right) \div n\right]-(2 n \div n) {/tex}
{tex} =a-2 {/tex}
Therefore, the new average is 2 less than the original average.

Q 15

Try to explain this using the fair-share interpretation of average that you learnt last year.

Solution

Using the fair-share interpretation:
If everyone has their fair share (the average), and then everyone gives away 2 units, the new fair share will be the old fair share minus 2.
For example: If 4 friends share 20 chocolates equally, each gets 5 chocolates (fair share {tex}=5{/tex}).
If each friend gives away 2 chocolates, they now have {tex}20-(4 \times 2)=12{/tex} chocolates total.
New fair share {tex}=12 \div 4=3=5-2{/tex}
The fair share decreased by exactly 2.

Q 16

What happens to the average if every value in the collection is doubled?

Solution

The average also doubles.
Example: Consider data: 3, 5, 7
Original mean {tex}=(3+5+7) \div 3=15 \div 3=5{/tex}
Doubled data: 6, 10, 14
New mean {tex}=(6+10+14) \div 3=30 \div 3=10{/tex}
New mean {tex}=2 \times{/tex} Original mean
Algebraic Proof:
Let the {tex}n{/tex} values be {tex}x_1, x_2, x_3, \ldots x_n{/tex} with average {tex}a{/tex}.
Original average: {tex}\left(x_1+x_2+x_3+\ldots+x_n\right) \div n=a{/tex}
When every value is multiplied by 5:
New average {tex}=\left[\left(5 x_1\right)+\left(5 x_2\right)+\left(5 x_3\right)+\ldots+\left(5 x_n\right)\right] \div n{/tex}
{tex} =\left[5\left(x_1+x_2+x_3+\ldots+x_n\right)\right] \div n{/tex}{tex} \text { (using distributive property) } {/tex}
{tex} =5 \times\left[\left(x_1+x_2+x_3+\ldots+x_n\right) \div n\right] {/tex}
{tex} =5 \times a {/tex}
{tex} =5 a {/tex}
Therefore, when every value is multiplied by 5, the new average is 5 times the original average.
Similarly, when every value is doubled, the average also doubles.

sections.label: Tinkering with Median
sections.html:
Q 1

Will including a new value to the data increase or decrease the median?

Solution

Let's consider the data: {tex}3,5,7,8,10,11,13{/tex}
The median is 8 (middle value).
Case 1: Including a value greater than the median (e.g., 11)
New data: 3, 5, 7, 8, 10, 11, 11, 13
Since we now have 8 values (even), the median {tex}={/tex} average of 4th and 5th values Median {tex}= (8+10) \div 2=9{/tex}
The median increased from 8 to 9.
Case 2: Including a value less than the median(e.g.,4)
New data: 3, 4, 5, 7, 8, 10, 11, 13
Median {tex}=(7+8) \div 2=7.5{/tex}
The median decreased from 8 to 7.5.
Conclusion:

  • Including a value greater than the median {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} median increases
  • Including a value less than the median {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} median decreases
  • Including a value equal to the median {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} median stays the same (usually)

sections.label: Finding the Unknown
sections.html:
Q 1

Coach Balwan noted down the weights of the kushti players (wrestlers) and the mean as shown. But one value that was written down got smudged. Can you find out the missing value?
Given: Weights: 42, 40, 39, 33, 48, 38, 42, 35, 32, w
Mean = 39.2 kg

Solution

We know: Mean = Sum of all values {tex}\div{/tex} Number of values
{tex} 39.2=(42+40+39+33+48{/tex}{tex}+38+42+35+32+w) \div 10 {/tex}
{tex} 39.2=(349+w) \div 10 {/tex}
Multiplying both sides by 10:
{tex} 392=349+w {/tex}
{tex} w=392-349=43 {/tex}
The missing value is {tex}{4 3 ~ k g}{/tex}.

Q 2

Venkayya keeps track of the coconut harvest in his farm. He calculates the average harvest per tree as 25.6 . His son verifies the counts and finds that one tree's harvest count is incorrectly noted as 3 more than the actual number. Can you find the correct average if the number of trees is 15?

Solution

Given:

  • Number of trees {tex}=15{/tex}
  • Incorrect average {tex}=25.6{/tex}
  • One tree's count is 3 more than actual

Step 1: Find the incorrect total harvest
Average {tex}={/tex} Total harvest {tex}\div{/tex} Number of trees
{tex}25.6={/tex} Total harvest {tex}\div 15{/tex}
Total harvest (incorrect) {tex}=25.6 \times 15=384{/tex} coconuts
Step 2: Find the correct total harvest
Since one tree's count is 3 more than actual:
Correct total {tex}=384-3=381{/tex} coconuts
Step 3: Find the correct average
Correct average {tex}=381 \div 15=25.4{/tex}
The correct average harvest is 25.4 coconuts per tree.

sections.label: Mean and Median with Frequencies
sections.html:
Q 1

What is the average family size of students in your class? How would you find this out?

Number Frequency
3 3
4 11
5 9
6 7
7 3
8 1
9 1
10 1
Solution

Incorrect approach: {tex}(3+4+5+6+7{/tex}{tex}+8+9+10) \div 8=6.5{/tex}
This is wrong because it doesn't account for how many times each value occurs.
Correct approach:
Mean {tex}={/tex} Sum of all values ÷ Total number of values
Sum of all values {tex}=(3 \times 3)+(4 \times 11)+(5 \times 9)+{/tex}{tex}(6 \times 7)+(7 \times 3)+(8 \times 1){/tex}{tex}+(9 \times 1)+(10 \times 1){/tex}
{tex} =9+44+45+42+21+8+9+10 {/tex}
{tex} \text { = } 188 {/tex}
Total number of values {tex}=3+11+9+7+3+1+1+1=36{/tex}
{tex} \text { Mean }=188 \div 36=5.22 \text { (approximately) } {/tex}
The average family size is 5.22.

Q 2

What is the median family size of this class?

Solution

Total number of students {tex}=36{/tex}
Since 36 is even,the median will bethe average ofthe 18th and 19th values when data is arranged in order.
Let's add frequencies progressively:

  • Values 1 to 3: family size 3 (frequency 3)
  • Values 4 to 14: family size 4 (frequency 11, cumulative {tex}=14{/tex})
  • Values 15 to 23: family size 5 (frequency 9, cumulative {tex}=23{/tex})

The 18th and 19th values both fall inthe range where family size {tex}=5{/tex}.
Therefore, median {tex}={5}{/tex}.

Q 3

Can you tell which cell has the marks obtained by Farooq in Mathematics?

Solution

Farooq's marks in Mathematics are in cell E5.
(Column E = Mathematics, Row 5 = Farooq)

Q 4

Can you tell what data is in column B7?

Solution

Column B is for Odia marks, and Row 7 is for Gowri.
Therefore, cell B7 contains Gowri's marks in Odia = 27.

Q 5

 In which subjects has Ashwin scored more than 30 marks?

Solution

Ashwin is in Row 4. Let's check his marks:

  • Odia: 29 (not > 30)
  • Telugu: 31 (> 30)
  • English: 33 (> 30)
  • Maths: 34 (> 30)
  • Social Science: 30 (not > 30)
  • Science: 28 (not > 30)

Ashwin scored more than 30 marks in Telugu, English, and Maths.

Q 6

What formula would you type to find out the class average marks in Science?

Solution

Science marks are in column G, from rows 2 to 23.
Formula: = AVERAGE(G2:G23)
This will calculate the average of all Science marks in the class.

Q 7

Find out if the class average marks in Odia is greater than the class average marks in Telugu.

Solution

Formula for Odia average: = AVERAGE(B2:B23) Formula for Telugu average:
= AVERAGE(C2:C23)
When calculated:

  • Odia average {tex}\approx 30.23{/tex}
  • Telugu average {tex}\approx 32.68{/tex}

No, the class average marks in Odia (30.23) is less than the class average marks in Telugu (32.68).

sections.label: Figure it Out 1
sections.html:
Q 1

Find the mean of the following data and share your observations:

  1. The first 50 natural numbers.
  2. The first 50 odd numbers.
  3. The first 50 multiples of 4.
Solution
  1. The first 50 natural numbers are: {tex}1,2,3,4, \ldots, 50{/tex}
    Sum of first n natural numbers {tex}={n}({n}+1) \div 2{/tex}
    {tex} \text { Sum }=50 \times 51 \div 2=2550 \div 2=1275 {/tex}
    {tex} \text { Mean }=1275 \div 50=25.5 {/tex}
    Observation: The mean of the first {tex}{n}{/tex} natural numbers is always {tex}({n}+{1}) \div 2{/tex}. For 50 numbers, it's {tex}51 \div 2=25.5{/tex}.
  2. The first 50 odd numbers are: {tex}1,3,5,7, \ldots, 99{/tex}
    Sum of first {tex}{n}{/tex} odd numbers {tex}={n}^2{/tex}
    Sum {tex}=50^2=2500{/tex}
    {tex} \text { Mean }=2500 \div 50=50 {/tex}
    Observation: The mean of the first {tex}n{/tex} odd numbers is always {tex}n{/tex}. For 50 odd numbers, the mean is 50.
  3. The first 50 multiples of 4 are: {tex}4,8,12,16, \ldots, 200{/tex}
    This is 4 times the first 50 natural numbers.
    Sum {tex}=4 \times({/tex}sum of first 50 natural numbers{tex})=4 \times 1275=5100{/tex}
    Mean {tex}=5100 \div 50=102{/tex}
    Observation: The mean of the first {tex}{n}{/tex} multiples of 4 is 4 times the mean of the first {tex}{n}{/tex} natural numbers. Mean {tex}=4 \times 25.5=102{/tex}.
Q 2

The dot plot below shows a collection of data and its average; but one dot is missing. Mark the missing value so that the mean is 9 (as shown below).

Solution

From the dot plot, visible values are: {tex}7,8,8,9,10,11,12{/tex}
Let the missing value be x.
Given: Mean {tex}=9{/tex}
{tex} 9=(7+8+8+9+10+11+12+x) \div 8 {/tex}
{tex} 9=(65+x) \div 8 {/tex}
{tex} 72=65+x {/tex}
{tex} x=72-65=7 {/tex}
The missing value is 7
The missing dot should be marked at position 7 on the number line.

Q 3

Sudhakar, the class teacher, asks Shreyas to measure the heights of all 24 students in his class and calculate the average height. Shreyas informs the teacher that the average height is 150.2 cm. Sudhakar discovers that the students were wearing uniform shoes when the measurements were taken and the shoes add 1 cm to the height.

  1. Should the teacher get all the heights measured again without the shoes to find the correct average height? Or is there a simpler way?
  2. What is the correct average height of the class?
    1. 174.2 cm
    2. 126.2 cm
    3. 150.2 cm
    4. 149.2 cm
    5. 51.2 cm
    6. None of the above
    7. Insufficient information
Solution
  1. No, the teacher doesn't need to measure all heights again.
    Simpler way: Since every student was wearing shoes that add 1 cm to their height, every measurement is 1 cm more than it should be. Therefore, the average height is also 1 cm more than it should be.
    Correct average height = Measured average height - {tex}1 {~cm}=150.2-1=149.2 {~cm}{/tex}
  2. (d) 149.2 cm
    Explanation: When a fixed amount (1 cm) is added to every value, the average also increases by that same amount. Conversely, when we subtract 1 cm from every measurement, the average also decreases by 1 cm.
Q 4

The three dot plots below show the lengths, in minutes, of songs of different albums. Which of these has a mean of 5.57 minutes? Explain how you arrived at the answer.

Solution

To find which album has a mean of 5.57 minutes, we need to calculate the mean for each dot plot
Album A: Let's count the dots and their positions:

  • At 4: 2 dots
  • At 5: 5 dots
  • At 6: 3 dots
  • At 7: 2 dots

{tex} 5 {um}=(4 \times 2)+(5 \times 5)+{/tex}{tex}(6 \times 3)+(7 \times 2)=8+25+18+14=65 {/tex}
Number of songs {tex}=2+5+3+2=12{/tex}
Mean {tex}=65 \div 12=5.42{/tex} minutes (approximately)
Album B:

  • At 3 : 1 dot
  • At 4 : 2 dots
  • At 5 : 3dots
  • At 6 : 4 dots
  • At 7 : 4 dots

{tex} 5 {um}=(3 \times 1)+(4 \times 2)+{/tex}{tex}(5 \times 3)+(6 \times 4)+{/tex}{tex}(7 \times 4)=3+8+15+24+28=78 {/tex}
Number of songs {tex}=1+2+3+4+4=14{/tex}
Mean {tex}=78 \div 14=5.57{/tex} minutes
Album C:

  • At 4: 4 dots
  • At 5: 3dots
  • At 6:4 dots
  • At 7: 1 dot

{tex}{Sum}=(4 \times 4)+(5 \times 3)+{/tex}{tex}(6 \times 4)+(7 \times 1){/tex} {tex}=16+15+24+7=62{/tex}
Number of songs {tex}=4+3+4+1=12{/tex}
Mean {tex}=62 \div 12=5.17{/tex} minutes (approximately)
Answer: Album {tex}B{/tex} has a mean of 5.57 minutes.

Q 5

Find the median of {tex}8,10,19,23,26,34,40,41,41,48,51,55,70{/tex}, 84, 91, 92.

  1. If we include one value to the data (in the given list) without affecting the median, what could that value be?
  2. If we include two values to the data without affecting the median what could the two values be?
  3. If we remove one value from the data without affecting the median what could the value be?
Solution

The data is already in ascending order.
Number of values {tex}=16{/tex} (even)
Median {tex}={/tex} Average of 8th and 9th values
8 th value {tex}=41{/tex} th value {tex}=41{/tex}
Median {tex}=(41+41) \div 2=82 \div 2=41{/tex}

  1. To keep the median at 41, the new value should be 41 itself.
    When we add 41, the data becomes: {tex}8,10,19,23,26,34,40,41{/tex}{tex},41,41,48,51,55,70{/tex}, 84,91,92 (17 values)
    The 9th value (middle value) is still 41.
    Median remains 41
  2. We can include any two values such that one is {tex}\leq 41{/tex} and the other is {tex}\geq 41{/tex}.
    Example 1: Include 30 and 50 New data: {tex}8,10,19,23,26,30,34,40{/tex}{tex},41,41,48,50,51{/tex}, {tex}55,70,84,91,92(18{/tex} values{tex}){/tex}
    Median {tex}=(41+41) \div 2=41{/tex}
    Example 2: Include 41 and 41
    New data has 18 values with 9th and 10th both being 41
    Median = 41
  3. We can remove any value except 41.
    Example: Remove 8
    New data: {tex}10,19,23,26,34,40,41,{/tex}{tex}41,48,51,55,70,84,91,92{/tex} (15 values)
    Median {tex}=8{/tex}th
    value {tex}=41{/tex}
    Or remove 92 : New data: {tex}8,10,19,23,26,34,40,{/tex}{tex}41,41,48,51,55,70,84,91{/tex} (15 values) Median {tex}=8{/tex}th
    value {tex}=41{/tex}
Q 6

Examine the statements below and justify if the statement is always true, sometimes true, or never true.

  1. Removing a value less than the median will decrease the median.
  2. Including a value less than the mean will decrease the mean.
  3. Including any 4 values will not affect the median.
  4. Including 4 values less than the median will increase the median.
Solution
  1. Sometimes true.
    Example when true: Data: {tex}1,2,3,4,5{/tex} (median = 3)
    Remove 1: New data: {tex}2,3,4,5{/tex} (median = 3.5)
    The median increased (not decreased), so this shows it's not always true.
    Example when false: Data: {tex}1,3,5,7,9{/tex} (median {tex}=5){/tex}
    Remove 1: New data: 3,5,7,9 {tex}({/tex}median {tex}=6){/tex}
    The median increased.
    Conclusion: The statement is sometimes true depending on the position and distribution of values.
  2. Always true.
    When we include a value less than the mean, the total sum increases by less than the mean amount, but the count increases by 1. This causes the average to decrease.
    Example: Data: {tex}4,6,8({/tex} mean {tex}=6){/tex} Include {tex}2({/tex} which is {tex}<6){/tex} : New data: {tex}2,4,6,8{/tex}
    New mean {tex}= 20 \div 4=5{/tex}
    The mean decreased from 6 to 5
  3. Sometimes true.
    Example when true: Data: {tex}10,20,30,40,50({/tex}median {tex}=30){/tex}
    Include: {tex}15,25,35,45{/tex}
    New data: {tex}10,15,20,25,30,35,40,45,50(9{/tex} value{tex}){/tex}
    Median {tex}=30{/tex}
    Example when false: Data: {tex}10,20,30{/tex} (median {tex}=20){/tex} Include: {tex}1,2,3,4 {/tex}
    New data: {tex}1,2,3,4{/tex}, 10,20,30 (7 values)
    Median = 4 (changed!)
    Conclusion: Sometimes true, depending on what values are included.
  4. Never true.
    Including values less than the median will either keep the median the same or decrease it, but it will never increase it.
    Example: Data: 10,20,30,40,50 (median = 30)
    Include: 5,8,12,15 (all < 30)
    New data: 5, {tex}8,10,12,15,20,30,40,50(9{/tex} values{tex}){/tex}
    Median {tex}=15({/tex} decreased! {tex}){/tex}
Q 7

The mean of the numbers 8, 13, 10, 4, 5, 20, y, 10 is 10.375. Find the value of y.

Solution

Mean {tex}={/tex} Sum of all values {tex}\div{/tex} Number of values
{tex} 10.375=(8+13+10+4{/tex}{tex}+5+20+y+10) \div 8 {/tex}
{tex} 10.375=(70+y) \div 8 {/tex}
Multiplying both sides by 8:
{tex} 83=70+y {/tex}
{tex} y=83-70=13 {/tex}
The value of y is 13.
Verification: {tex}(8+13+10+4+5+20+13+10){/tex}{tex} \div 8=83 \div 8=10.375{/tex}

Q 8

The mean of a set of data with 15 values is 134. Find the sum of the data.

Solution

{tex}\text { Mean }=\frac{\text { Sum of all values }}{\text { Number of values }}{/tex}
{tex}134=\frac{\text { Sum of all values }}{15}{/tex}
{tex}134 \times 15=\text { Sum of all values }{/tex}
{tex}2010=\text { Sum of all values }{/tex}
{tex}\text { Sum of all values }={2 0 1 0}{/tex}

Q 9

Consider the data: {tex}12,47,8,73,18,35,39,8,29,25, p{/tex}. Which of the following number(s) could be {tex}p{/tex} if the median of this data is 29?

  1. 10
  2. 25
  3. 40
  4. 100
  5. 29
  6. 47
  7. 30
Solution

The data has 11 values, sothe median will bethe 6th value when arranged in order.
First, let's arrangethe known values: {tex}8,8,12,18,25,29,35,39,47,73{/tex}
We needthe 6th value to be 29.
Currently without p, in order: {tex}8,8,12,18,25,29,35,39,47,73{/tex}
Testing each option:

  1. {tex}{p}={1 0 : A rr a n g e d}:{/tex}{tex} 8,8,10,12,18,25,29,35,39,47,73{/tex} 
    6th value {tex}=25{/tex}
  2. {tex}{p}={2 5}{/tex} :Arranged: {tex}8,8,12,18,25,25,29,35,39,47,73{/tex}
    6th value {tex}=25{/tex}
  3. {tex}{p}={4 0}{/tex} :Arranged: {tex}8,8,12,18,25,29,35,39,40,47,73{/tex}
    6th value {tex}=29{/tex}
  4. {tex}{p}={1 0 0}:{/tex} Arranged: {tex}8,8,12,18,25,29,35,39,47,73,100{/tex}
    6th value {tex}=29{/tex}
  5. p = 29:Arranged: {tex}8,8,12,18,25,29,29,35,39,47,73{/tex}
    6th value {tex}=29{/tex}
  6. {tex}{p}={4 7}:{/tex} Arranged: {tex}8,8,12,18,25,29,35,39,47,47,73{/tex}
    6th value {tex}=29{/tex}
  7. p = 30:Arranged: {tex}8,8,12,18,25,29,30,35,39,47,73{/tex}
    6th value {tex}=29{/tex}

Answer: (iii) 40, (iv) 100, (v) 29, (vi) 47, (vii) 30
All these values keepthe median at 29 because they are all {tex}\geq 29{/tex}, sothe 6th position remains 29.

Q 10

The number of times students rode their cycles in a week is shown in the dot plot below. Four students rode their cycles twice in that week.

  1. Find the average number of times students rode their cycles.
  2. Find the median number of times students rode their cycles.
  3. Which of the following statements are valid? Why?
    1. Everyone used their cycle at least once.
    2. Almost everyone used their cycle a few times.
    3. There are some students who cycled more than once on some days.
    4. Exactly 5 students have used their cycles more than once on some days.
    5. The following week, if all of them cycled 1 more time than they did the previous week, what would be the average and median of the next week’s data?
Solution
  1. From the dot plot:
    • 0 times: 2 students
    • 1 time: 3 students
    • 2 times: 4 students
    • 3 times: 5 students
    • 4 times: 3 students
    • 5 times: 2 students
    • 6 times: 1 student
    Total students {tex}=2+3+4+5+3+2+1=20{/tex}
    Sum {tex}=(0 \times 2)+(1 \times 3)+(2 \times 4)+(3 \times 5)+{/tex}{tex}(4 \times 3)+(5 \times 2)+(6 \times 1){/tex}{tex}=0+3+8+15+12+10+ 6=54{/tex}
    Average {tex}=54 \div 20={2 . 7}{/tex} times
  2. Number of students = 20 (even)
    Median = Average of 10th and 11th values
    Counting from the dot plot in order:
    • Positions 1-2 : 0 times
    • Positions 3-5 : 1 time
    • Positions 6-9 : 2 times
    • Positions 10-14 : 3 times
    The 10th and 11th values are both 3.
    Median {tex}=(3+3) \div 2={/tex} 3 times
    1. Not valid.
      From the dot plot, 2 students rode their cycles 0 times. So, not everyone used their cycle at least once.
    2. Valid.
