  • After round {tex}1: 2(7)-\mathrm{c}=14-\mathrm{c}{/tex}
  • After round 2: {tex}2(14-c)-c=28-3 c{/tex}
  • After round {tex}3: 2(28-3 c)-c=56-7 c{/tex}

For Karim to increase his coins: {tex}56-7 \mathrm{c}>7{/tex}

  • {tex}56-7>7 \mathrm{c}{/tex}
  • {tex}49>7 c{/tex}
  • {tex}\mathrm{c}<7{/tex}

Answer: The cost per round should be less than 7 coins.
For example, if {tex}\mathrm{c}=6{/tex} :

  • After 3 rounds: {tex}56-7(6)=56-42=14{/tex} coins (doubled his money!)

(iii) Let Karim start with n coins, and let {tex}\mathrm{c}={/tex} cost per round.
After 3 rounds, Karim has: 8n-7c coins
For the genie to get all coins: {tex}8 n-7 c=0{/tex}

  • {tex}7 \mathrm{c}=8 \mathrm{n}{/tex}
  • {tex}\mathrm{c}=8 \mathrm{n} / 7{/tex}

Answer: The genie should charge {tex}(\mathbf{8 n}) / \mathbf{7}{/tex} coins per round, where {tex}\mathbf{n}{/tex} is Karim's starting amount.
For this to be a whole number, n must be a multiple of 7.
General formula: If Karim has {tex}n{/tex} coins initially, the genie should charge {tex}8 n / 7{/tex} coins per round.
For {tex}\mathrm{n}=7: \mathrm{c}=8(7) / 7=8{/tex} coins per round (as in the original story)
For {tex}\mathrm{n}=14: \mathrm{c}=8(14) / 7=16{/tex} coins per round
For {tex}\mathrm{n}=21: \mathrm{c}=8(21) / 7=24{/tex} coins per round

sections.label: Why Can't Perimeter be a Measure of Area?
sections.html:
Q 1

Why do we count the number of unit squares to assign measures for area? Couldn’t we have just used the perimeter of a region as a measure of its area?

Solution

We count unit squares to measure area because area depends on the surface covered, not just the boundary.
Perimeter only measures the length of the boundary, not how much region is enclosed.
Two regions can have the same perimeter but different areas, so perimeter cannot correctly represent area.

Q 2

If two regions have the same perimeter, can’t we conclude that they have the same area? Or, if one region has a larger perimeter than another, can’t we conclude that it also has a larger area?

Solution

No, we cannot conclude that.

  • Two regions may have the same perimeter but different areas.
  • A region can have a larger perimeter but smaller area than another region.

Hence, perimeter is not indicative of area.

Q 3

Also, give an example of two regions of other shapes where the region with the larger perimeter has the smaller area! This property should be visually clear in your example.

Solution

Consider:

  • A long, thin zig-zag shaped region
  • A compact circular region

The zig-zag shape has a very large boundary (perimeter) but covers very little area.
The circle has a smaller perimeter but covers a much larger area.
Thus, a region with a larger perimeter can have a smaller area, which is visually clear.

sections.label: Figure it Out
sections.html:
Q 1

Identify the missing sidelengths.



Solution
  1. For the first rectangle:
    • Area {tex}=14 \ \mathrm{in}^2{/tex}
    • One side {tex}=7\ \mathrm{in}{/tex}
    • Missing side {tex}={/tex} Area {tex}\div{/tex} Known side {tex}=14 \div 7=\mathbf{2}{/tex} in
    For the second rectangle:
    • Area {tex}=35 \mathrm{in}^2{/tex}
    • One side {tex}=7\ \mathrm{in}{/tex}
    • Missing side {tex}=35 \div 7=5{/tex} in
    For the third rectangle:
    • Area {tex}=28 \mathrm{in}^2{/tex}
    • One side {tex}=4{/tex} in
    • Missing side {tex}=28 \div 4=7{/tex} in
    For the fourth rectangle:
    •  Area {tex}=21 \mathrm{in}^2{/tex}
    • One side {tex}=3 \mathrm{in}{/tex}
    • Missing side {tex}=21 \div 3=7{/tex} in
  2. Total area = 50 m2.
    For the left rectangle:
    • One side {tex}=4 \mathrm{~m}{/tex}
    • Area {tex}=29 \mathrm{~m}^2{/tex}
    • Missing side {tex}=29 \div 4=7.25 \mathrm{~m}{/tex}
    For the right rectangle:
    • Area {tex}=50-29=21 \mathrm{~m}^2{/tex}
    • We need more information. If we assume the right rectangle shares a side with the left rectangle (7.25 m), then:
    • Missing side {tex}=21 \div 7.25 \approx \mathbf{2 . 9 m}{/tex}
    Alternatively, if the total width is needed:
    • Total width sharing the 4 m height: Missing side for right rectangle {tex}=11 \div 4=\mathbf{2 . 7 5 ~ m}{/tex}
Q 2

The figure shows a path (the shaded portion) laid around a rectangular park EFGH.

  1. What measurements do you need to find the area of the path? Once you identify the lengths to be measured, assign possible values of your choice to these measurements and find the area of the path. Give a formula for the area.
    An example of a formula — Area of a rectangle = length × width. [Hint: There is a relation between the areas of EFGH, the path, and ABCD.]
  2. If the width of the path along each side is given, can you find its area? If not, what other measurements do you need? Assign values of your choice to these measurements and find the area of the path. Give a formula for the area using these measurements.
    [Hint: Break the path into rectangles.]
  3. Does the area of the path change when the outer rectangle is moved while keeping the inner rectangular park EFGH inside it, as shown?
Solution
  1. Measurements needed:
    • Length of outer rectangle ABCD (let's call it L )
    • Width of outer rectangle ABCD (let's call it W )
    • Length of inner rectangle EFGH (let's call it I)
    • Width of inner rectangle EFGH (let's call it w)
      Let's assign values:
    • {tex}\mathrm{L}=20 \mathrm{~m}, \mathrm{~W}=15 \mathrm{~m}{/tex}
    • {tex}\mathrm{l}=16 \mathrm{~m}, \mathrm{w}=11 \mathrm{~m}{/tex}

      Calculation: Area of path {tex}={/tex} Area of ABCD - Area of EFGH {tex}=(\mathrm{L} \times \mathrm{W})-(\mathrm{I} \times \mathrm{w})=(20 \times 15)-(16 \times 11)=300 -176=\mathbf{1 2 4} \mathbf{m}^{\mathbf{2}}{/tex}

      Formula: Area of path {tex}=(\mathrm{L} \times \mathrm{W})-(\mathrm{I} \times \mathrm{w}){/tex}
      where {tex}L{/tex} and {tex}W{/tex} are the length and width of the outer rectangle, and {tex}I{/tex} and w are the length and width of the inner rectangle.

  2. Yes, if the width of the path is uniform along each side, we can find its area, but we also need the dimensions of either the inner or outer rectangle.
    Let's say:
    • Width of path {tex}=d=2 m{/tex} (uniform on all sides)
    • Length of inner park EFGH {tex}=\mathrm{I}=16 \mathrm{~m}{/tex}
    • Width of inner park EFGH {tex}=\mathrm{w}=11 \mathrm{~m}{/tex}
      Then:
    • Length of outer rectangle {tex}=1+2 d=16+2(2)=20 m{/tex}
    • Width of outer rectangle {tex}=w+2 d=11+2(2)=15 m{/tex}
      Breaking the path into rectangles:
      The path can be divided into 4 rectangles:
    • Top rectangle: {tex}(1+2 \mathrm{~d}) \times \mathrm{d}=20 \times 2=40 \mathrm{~m}^2{/tex}
    • Bottom rectangle: {tex}(1+2 d) \times d=20 \times 2=40 \mathrm{~m}^2{/tex}
    • Left rectangle: {tex}\mathrm{w} \times \mathrm{d}=11 \times 2=22 \mathrm{~m}^2{/tex}
    • Right rectangle: {tex}\mathrm{w} \times \mathrm{d}=11 \times 2=22 \mathrm{~m}^2{/tex}

      Total area of path {tex}=40+40+22+22=\mathbf{1 2 4} \mathbf{~ m}^{\mathbf{2}}{/tex}
      Alternative formula: Area of path {tex}=(l+2 d)(w+2 d)-l w=2 d(l+w)+4 d^2=2(2)(16+11)+4(2)^2=4(27)+ 16=108+16=\mathbf{1 2 4} \mathbf{m}^{\mathbf{2}}{/tex}
      Formula: Area of path {tex}\mathbf{= 2 d}(\mathbf{I}+\mathbf{w})+\mathbf{4 d}^{\mathbf{2}}{/tex}
      where {tex}d{/tex} is the width of the path, {tex}I{/tex} is the length of inner park, and {tex}w{/tex} is the width of inner park.

  3. No, the area of the path does not change when the outer rectangle is moved while keeping the inner rectangular park EFGH inside it.
    Reason: The area of the path depends only on:
    • Area of outer rectangle ABCD
    • Area of inner rectangle EFGH

      Area of path = Area of ABCD - Area of EFGH
      As long as the dimensions of both rectangles remain the same, the area of the path remains constant, regardless of the position of the inner rectangle within the outer rectangle.

      The path may have different shapes in different positions, but its total area remains unchanged.

Q 3

The figure shows a plot with sides 14 m and 12 m, and with a crosspath. What other measurements do you need to find the area of the crosspath? Once you identify the lengths to be measured, assign some possible values of your choice and find the area of the path. Give a formula for the area based on the measurements you choose.

Solution

Measurements needed:

  • Length of plot {tex}=14 \mathrm{~m}{/tex} (given)
  • Width of plot {tex}=12 \mathrm{~m}{/tex} (given)
  • Width of horizontal path {tex}=\mathrm{w}_1{/tex} (to be assigned)
  • Width of vertical path = {tex}\mathrm{w}_2{/tex} (to be assigned)

Let's assign values:

  • {tex}\mathrm{w}_1=2 \mathrm{~m}{/tex} (width of horizontal path)
  • {tex}\mathrm{w}_2=2 \mathrm{~m}{/tex} (width of vertical path)

Calculation:
The crosspath consists of two rectangles that overlap in the middle.
Method 1: Add both paths and subtract the overlapping square:

  • Area of horizontal path {tex}=14 \times 2=28 \mathrm{m}^2{/tex}
  • Area of vertical path {tex}=12 \times 2=24 \mathrm{m}^2{/tex}
  • Area of overlapping square {tex}=2 \times 2=4 \mathrm{m}^2{/tex}

Area of crosspath {tex}=28+24-4=\mathbf{4 8} \mathbf{m}^{\mathbf{2}}{/tex}
Formula: Area of crosspath {tex}\mathbf{=}\left(\mathbf{L} \times \mathbf{w}_{\mathbf{1}}\right) \mathbf{+}\left(\mathbf{W} \times \mathbf{w}_{\mathbf{2}}\right)-\left(\mathbf{w}_{\mathbf{1}} \times \mathbf{w}_{\mathbf{2}}\right){/tex}
where {tex}L{/tex} is the length of plot, {tex}W{/tex} is the width of plot, {tex}w_1{/tex} is the width of horizontal path, and {tex}\mathrm{w}_2{/tex} is the width of vertical path.

Q 4

Find the area of the spiral tube shown in the figure. The tube has the same width throughout.

[Hint: There are different ways of finding the area. Here is one method.]

What should be the length of the straight tube if it is to have the same area as the bent tube on the left?

Solution

From the figure, we can see that the tube has a uniform width of 5 units throughout.
Method 1: Uncoiling the spiral
If we imagine straightening out the spiral tube into a single straight tube, we need to find its total length.
Looking at the measurements:

  • The spiral has dimensions {tex}20 \times 20{/tex} units
  • Inner measurements show {tex}15,10,5{/tex} units at various points
  • The tube width is 5 units

Let's trace the path of the tube from outside to inside:

  • Outer horizontal length: 20 units
  • Right vertical length: 20 units
  • Bottom horizontal length: 15 units
  • Left vertical length: 15 units
  • Inner horizontal length: 10 units
  • Right vertical length: 10 units
  • Bottom horizontal length: 5 units

Total length of centerline {tex}=20+20+15+15+10+10+5=95{/tex} units
Area of spiral tube {tex}={/tex} Length {tex}\times{/tex} Width {tex}=95 \times 5=\mathbf{4 7 5}{/tex} square units

Q 5

In this figure, if the sidelength of the square is doubled, what is the increase in the areas of the regions 1, 2 and 3? Give reasons.

Solution

Let the original side length of the square be {tex}\mathbf{s}{/tex}.
Original areas: The figure shows a square divided into 4 regions by its diagonals.

  • Region 1, 2 and 3 each appear to be triangular regions
  • Original area of entire square {tex}=s^2{/tex}

When diagonals divide a square into 4 parts, each part has area {tex}=s^2 / 4{/tex}
After doubling the side length: New side length {tex}=2 s{/tex} New area of entire square {tex}=(2 s)^2=4 s^2{/tex}
Each region in the new square will have area {tex}=4 s^2 / 4=s^2{/tex}
Increase in area: For each region (1, 2 or 3):

  • Original area {tex}=s^2 / 4{/tex}
  • New area {tex}=\mathrm{s}^2{/tex}
  • Increase {tex}=s^2-s^2 / 4=3 s^2 / 4{/tex}

Ratio of increase: The area becomes 4 times the original area. So each region increases by a factor of 4.

If region 1 originally had area {tex}\mathrm{A}_1{/tex}, region 2 had area {tex}\mathrm{A}_2{/tex}, and region 3 had area {tex}\mathrm{A}_3{/tex}, then:

  • New area of region {tex}1=4 \mathrm{~A}_1{/tex}, increase {tex}=3 \mathrm{~A}_1{/tex}
  • New area of region {tex}2=4 \mathrm{~A}_2{/tex}, increase {tex}=3 \mathrm{~A}_2{/tex}
  • New area of region {tex}3=4 \mathrm{~A}_3{/tex}, increase {tex}=3 \mathrm{~A}_3{/tex}

Reason: When the side length is doubled, the area becomes 4 times (since area {tex}\propto{/tex} side2). Therefore, each region's area becomes 4 times its original area, representing an increase of 3 times the original area.

Q 6

Divide a square into 4 parts by drawing two perpendicular lines inside the square as shown in the figure. Rearrange the pieces to get a larger square, with a hole inside.

Solution

This is a hands-on activity. Here's how to do it:
Step 1: Take a square of cardboard or chart paper (say {tex}8 \mathrm{~cm} \times 8 \mathrm{~cm}{/tex}).
Step 2: Draw two perpendicular lines inside the square (not through the center), dividing it into 4 rectangular pieces.
Step 3: Cut along these lines to get 4 pieces.
Step 4: Rearrange these 4 pieces:

  • Place them at the four corners of a larger imaginary square
  • The pieces should be arranged so that they leave a square hole in the middle

Verification: Let's say the original square has side '{tex}a{/tex}', and you draw lines at distances {tex}' x{/tex}' and '{tex}y{/tex}' from the edges.
When rearranged properly:

  • The 4 pieces occupy the corners of a larger square
  • The area remains the same: {tex}a^2{/tex}
  • A square hole is created in the center

Example: If you have a {tex}10 \mathrm{~cm} \times 10 \mathrm{~cm}{/tex} square and draw perpendicular lines 3 cm from one edge and 4 cm from an adjacent edge, you'll get 4 rectangles that can be rearranged to form a larger square with a hole.
This activity demonstrates that the same area can be rearranged into different shapes!

sections.label: Triangles
sections.html:
Q 1

In the given figure, which triangle has a greater area: {tex}\triangle{/tex}XDC or {tex}\triangle{/tex}YDC, if both the rectangles are identical?

Solution

Both triangles have equal area.
Reason:

  • Both rectangles ABCD are identical
  • {tex}\triangle {XDC}{/tex} has base DC and height from X to DC (which is the height of rectangle AB )
  • {tex}\triangle {YDC}{/tex} has base DC and height from Y to DC (which is also the height of rectangle AB)

Since both triangles have the same base (DC) and the same height (height of rectangle), they have equal areas.

Area of {tex}\triangle {XDC}= \frac 1 2 \times {DC} \times {h}={/tex} Area of {tex}\triangle {YDC}{/tex}
where h is the height of the rectangle.
Each triangle has exactly half the area of rectangle {tex}A B C D{/tex}.

sections.label: Figure it Out 2
sections.html:
Q 1

Find the areas of the following triangles:

Solution
  1. Given: Triangle ABE with base {tex}\mathrm{BC}=4 \mathrm{~cm}{/tex} and height from A to {tex}\mathrm{BC}=3 \mathrm{~cm}{/tex}
    Area of triangle {tex}=1 / 2 \times{/tex} base {tex}\times{/tex} height {tex}=1 / 2 \times 4 \times 3=1 / 2 \times 12=6 \mathbf{c m}^{\mathbf{2}}{/tex}
  2. Given: Triangle DEF with base DF {tex}=3.2 \mathrm{~cm}{/tex} and height from E to {tex}\mathrm{DF}=5 \mathrm{~cm}{/tex}
    Area of triangle {tex}=1 / 2 \times{/tex} base {tex}\times{/tex} height {tex}=1 / 2 \times 3.2 \times 5=1 / 2 \times 16=8 \mathbf{c m}^2{/tex}
  3. Given: Triangle ANT with base {tex}\mathrm{AT}=4 \mathrm{~cm}{/tex} and height from N to {tex}\mathrm{AT}=3 \mathrm{~cm}{/tex}
    Area of triangle {tex}=1 / 2 \times{/tex} base {tex}\times{/tex} height {tex}=1 / 2 \times 4 \times 3=1 / 2 \times 12=6 \mathbf{c m}^{\mathbf{2}}{/tex}
Q 2

Find the length of the altitude BY.

Solution

Given from the figure:

  • Triangle ABC
  • {tex}\mathrm{AC}=8{/tex} units (base)
  • {tex}\mathrm{BX} \perp \mathrm{AC}, \mathrm{BX}=6{/tex} units (one altitude)
  • {tex}\mathrm{AB}=4{/tex} units
  • We need to find BY (altitude from B to AC along a different position)

Method: Using area of triangle
First, find the area using altitude BX: Area of {tex}\Delta \mathrm{ABC}=1 / 2 \times{/tex} base {tex}\times{/tex} height {tex}=1 / 2 \times \mathrm{AX} \times \mathrm{BX}{/tex}
We need to find {tex}A X{/tex} first or use the given information differently.
Actually, looking at the figure more carefully:

  • {tex}\mathrm{AC}=8{/tex} units (one side)
  • {tex}\mathrm{AB}=4{/tex} units (another side)
  • {tex}\mathrm{BX}=6{/tex} units (altitude to one side)

If BX is altitude to AB , then: Area {tex}=1 / 2 \times 4 \times 6=12{/tex} square units
Now, using AC as base: Area {tex}=1 / 2 \times \mathrm{AC} \times \mathrm{BY} 12{/tex}{tex}=1 / 2 \times 8 \times \mathrm{BY} 12=4 \times \mathrm{BYBY}{/tex}{tex}=12 \div 4 \mathrm{BY}=\mathbf{3}{/tex} units
Therefore, the length of altitude BY is {tex}\mathbf{3}{/tex} units.

Q 3

Find the area of {tex}\triangle{/tex}SUB, given that it is isosceles, SE is perpendicular to UB, and the area of {tex}\triangle{/tex}SEB is 24 sq. units.

Solution

Given:

  • {tex}\triangle \mathrm{SUB}{/tex} is isosceles
  • {tex}\mathrm{SE} \perp \mathrm{UB}{/tex}
  • Area of {tex}\triangle \mathrm{SEB}=24{/tex} sq. units

Since {tex}\triangle \mathrm{SUB}{/tex} is isosceles and SE is perpendicular to UB, SE must be the perpendicular bisector of UB (property of isosceles triangle - the altitude from the vertex angle bisects the base).

This means:

  • E is the midpoint of UB
  • {tex}\mathrm{UE}=\mathrm{EB}{/tex}
  • {tex}\triangle \mathrm{SUE}{/tex} and {tex}\triangle \mathrm{SEB}{/tex} are congruent triangles

Since the triangles are congruent: Area of {tex}\Delta \mathrm{SUE}={/tex} Area of {tex}\Delta \mathrm{SEB}=24{/tex} sq. units
Therefore: Area of {tex}\Delta \mathrm{SUB}={/tex} Area of {tex}\Delta \mathrm{SUE}+{/tex} Area of {tex}\Delta \mathrm{SEB}=24+24=\mathbf{4 8}{/tex} sq. units

Q 4

[Śulba-Sūtras] Give a method to transform a rectangle into a triangle of equal area.

Solution

Here's a simple method to transform a rectangle into a triangle of equal area:
Method:
Step 1: Take a rectangle {tex}A B C D{/tex} with length l and width W.
Step 2: Mark the midpoint {tex}M{/tex} of side {tex}A B{/tex}.
Step 3: Draw a triangle using:

  • Base {tex}=\mathrm{AB}{/tex} (same as rectangle's length = l)
  • Vertex at point E such that the perpendicular distance from E to AB equals 2 w (twice the width of rectangle)

Verification:

  • Area of rectangle {tex}=\mathrm{l} \times \mathrm{w}{/tex}
  • Area of triangle {tex}=1 / 2 \times{/tex} base × height {tex}=1 / 2 \times \mathrm{I} \times 2 \mathrm{w}=\mathrm{l} \times \mathrm{w}{/tex}

Alternative, simpler method:

Take one side of the rectangle as the base of the triangle (say length I), and make the height of the triangle equal to twice the width of the rectangle {tex}(2 \mathrm{w}){/tex}.

Q 5

[Śulba-Sūtras] Give a method to transform a triangle into a rectangle of equal area.

Solution

Here's a method to transform a triangle into a rectangle of equal area:
Method:
Step 1: Take a triangle {tex}A B C{/tex} with base {tex}b{/tex} and height {tex}h{/tex}.
Step 2: Find the midpoint M of the height (so the height is divided into two parts of {tex}\mathrm{h} / 2{/tex} each).
Step 3: Draw a line parallel to the base through M. This line intersects the sides of the triangle.
Step 4: Create a rectangle using:

  • Length = same as the triangle's base = b
  • Width {tex}={/tex} half of the triangle's height {tex}=\mathrm{h} / 2{/tex}

Verification:

  • Area of triangle {tex}=1 / 2 \times b \times h{/tex}
  • Area of rectangle {tex}=\mathrm{b} \times(\mathrm{h} / 2)=1 / 2 \times \mathrm{b} \times \mathrm{h}{/tex}

Practical method:

  1. Draw triangle {tex}A B C{/tex}
  2. Mark D and E as midpoints of sides AB and AC
  3. Draw DE parallel to BC
  4. {tex}\mathrm{DE}=1 / 2 \ {BC}{/tex} (mid-point theorem)
  5. Construct a rectangle with length BC and width equal to the distance from DE to BC
  6. This rectangle will have the same area as the triangle

The key principle is: if a triangle has base {tex}b{/tex} and height {tex}h{/tex}, a rectangle with base {tex}b{/tex} and height {tex}\mathrm{h} / 2{/tex} (or base {tex}\mathrm{b} / 2{/tex} and height h) will have the same area.

Q 6

ABCD, BCEF, and BFGH are identical squares.

  1. If the area of the red region is 49 sq. units, then what is the area of the blue region?
  2. In another version of this figure, if the total area enclosed by the blue and red regions is 180 sq. units, then what is the area of each square?
Solution

  1. Let the side of each square be 's'.
    Since all three squares are identical: Area of each square {tex}=s^2{/tex}
    Looking at the arrangement:

    • The three squares share vertices
    • Red region appears to be a triangle
    • Blue region appears to be another triangle
      If the red region (triangle) has area 49 sq. units:
    For identical squares arranged this way, when we connect specific vertices, we create congruent triangles due to symmetry.
    The red and blue regions are congruent triangles (by symmetry of the arrangement).
    Therefore: Area of blue region = {tex}{4 9}{/tex} sq. units

  2. Given: Total area of blue and red regions = 180 sq. units
    From part (i), we know that the blue and red regions have equal areas.
    So: Area of red region {tex}={/tex} Area of blue region
    Let area of each colored region {tex}=\mathrm{A}{/tex}
    Then: {tex}A+A=1802 A=180 A=90{/tex} sq. units
    Now, observing the geometric arrangement: The total colored area (red + blue) relates to the squares' areas.
    If three identical squares each have area {tex}\mathrm{s}^2{/tex}, and the colored regions represent specific fractions of these squares:
    Looking at the pattern, if the colored regions together equal 180 sq. units, and this represents a certain fraction of the total area of the squares:
    Let's say the colored area is half the total area of the three squares (this depends on the exact configuration):
    {tex}3 \mathrm{s}^2=2 \times 180=360 \mathrm{s}^2=120{/tex} sq. units
    But if the colored region equals the area of one square: {tex}s^2=90{/tex} sq. units
    Area of each square = 90 sq. units

Q 7

If M and N are the midpoints of XY and XZ, what fraction of the area of {tex}\triangle{/tex}XYZ is the area of {tex}\triangle{/tex}XMN?

Solution

Given:

  • M is the midpoint of XY
  • N is the midpoint of XZ
  • We need to find the ratio: Area of {tex}\Delta X M N{/tex} / Area of {tex}\Delta X Y Z{/tex}

Solution:
Following the hint, join NY.
Step 1: Since M and N are midpoints:

  • {tex}\mathrm{XM}=\frac 1 2 \mathrm{XY}{/tex}
  • {tex}X N=\frac{1}{2} X Z{/tex}

Step 2: {tex}\ln \triangle \mathrm{XMN}{/tex} and {tex}\triangle \mathrm{XYZ}{/tex}:

  • Both triangles share angle {tex}X{/tex}
  • {tex}X M / X Y=1 / 2{/tex} and {tex}X N / X Z=1 / 2{/tex}

By the Basic Proportionality Theorem (or Mid-point Theorem): MN is parallel to YZ and {tex}\mathrm{MN}=1 / 2 \mathrm{YZ}{/tex}

Step 3: Finding the area ratio:
The ratio of areas of similar triangles equals the square of the ratio of their corresponding sides.
Area of {tex}\triangle \mathrm{XMN} /{/tex} Area of {tex}\triangle \mathrm{XYZ}=(\mathrm{XM} / \mathrm{XY})^2=(1 / 2)^2=1 / 4{/tex}
Therefore: Area of {tex}\triangle \mathbf{X M N}=\mathbf{1} \mathbf{/ 4} \boldsymbol{\times}{/tex} Area of {tex}\boldsymbol{\triangle} \mathbf{X Y Z}{/tex}
The fraction is {tex}\mathbf{1} / \mathbf{4}{/tex} or one-fourth.
Verification using the hint: When we join NY, we create triangle XNY.

  • Area of {tex}\triangle X N Y=1 / 2 \times{/tex} Area of {tex}\triangle X Y Z{/tex} (since {tex}N{/tex} is midpoint of {tex}X Z{/tex})
  • Area of {tex}\triangle \mathrm{XMN}=1 / 2 \times{/tex} Area of {tex}\triangle \mathrm{XNY}{/tex} (since M is midpoint of XY)
  • Therefore, Area of {tex}\triangle \mathrm{XMN}=1 / 2 \times 1 / 2 \times{/tex} Area of {tex}\triangle \mathrm{XYZ}=1 / 4 \times{/tex} Area of {tex}\triangle \mathrm{XYZ}{/tex}
Q 8

Gopal needs to carry water from the river to his water tank. He starts from his house. What is the shortest path he can take from his house to the river and then to the water tank? Roughly recreate the map in your notebook and trace the shortest path.

Solution

This problem uses the reflection principle (mirror method) discussed in the textbook.
Method:
Step 1: Treat the river as a mirror line (line of reflection).
Step 2: Find the reflection of the water tank across the river. Let's call this point Water Tank' (WT').
Step 3: Draw a straight line from House to WT'. This line will intersect the river at some point, say {tex}P{/tex}.
Step 4: The shortest path is:

  • House {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} P (point on river) {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} Water Tank

Why this works:

  • The distance from House to P to Water Tank equals the distance from House to P to WT'
  • The shortest distance between two points (House and WT') is a straight line
  • By the law of reflection, the path House {tex}\rightarrow{/tex}{tex}\mathrm{P} \rightarrow{/tex} Water Tank has the same length as House {tex}\rightarrow \mathrm{P} \rightarrow \mathrm{WT}{ }^{\prime}{/tex}
  • This is the minimum possible distance

Steps to trace in notebook:

  1. Draw the river as a line
  2. Mark House on one side
  3. Mark Water Tank on the other side
  4. Reflect Water Tank across the river to get WT'
  5. Join House to WT' with a straight line
  6. Mark point P where this line crosses the river
  7. The path is: House {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\mathrm{P} \rightarrow{/tex} Water Tank (following the straight line to P , then from P to the actual Water Tank)

This ensures Gopal takes the shortest possible path!

sections.label: Area of any Polygon
sections.html:
Q 1

How do we find the area of this quadrilateral? What measurements do we need for this?
If we join BD, the quadrilateral ABCD gets divided into two triangles. By finding their areas, we can find the area of ABCD.

Solution

To find the area of quadrilateral {tex}A B C D{/tex}, we draw the diagonal {tex}B D{/tex}, which divides the quadrilateral into two triangles:
{tex}\triangle A B D{/tex} and {tex}\triangle B C D{/tex}.
Measurements needed:

  • Base and corresponding height of {tex}\triangle \mathrm{ABD}{/tex}
  • Base and corresponding height of {tex}\triangle \mathrm{BCD}{/tex}

Method:

  1. Join BD.
  2. Find the area of each triangle using the formula:
    {tex} \text { Area of triangle }=\frac{1}{2} \times \text { base × height } {/tex}
  3. Add the areas of the two triangles.

Area of quadrilateral {tex}\mathrm{ABCD}={/tex} Area of {tex}\triangle A B D+{/tex} Area of {tex}\triangle B C D{/tex}
This method helps us find the area of an irregular quadrilateral by dividing it into simpler shapes.

Q 2

How do we find the area of this pentagon?

Solution

To find the area of the given pentagon, we divide it into triangles by drawing diagonals from one vertex to the other non-adjacent vertices.

Each triangle's area is found using the formula:
{tex} =\frac{1}{2} \times \text { Area of a triangle }=21 \times { base \times height } {/tex}
After finding the areas of all the triangles, we add them together to get the area of the pentagon.
Thus, the area of a pentagon (or any polygon) can be found by:

  • Dividing it into triangles
  • Finding the area of each triangle
  • Adding all the triangle areas
Q 3

Can any polygon be divided into triangles?

Solution

It can be seen that any polygon can be divided into triangles. Thus, by knowing how to compute the area of a triangle, we can find the area of any polygon.

sections.label: Figure it Out 3
sections.html:
Q 1

Find the area of the quadrilateral ABCD given that AC = 22 cm, BM = 3 cm, DN = 3 cm, BM is perpendicular to AC, and DN is perpendicular to AC.

Solution

Yes, any polygon can be divided into a finite number of triangles by drawing diagonals from one vertex. Hence, by knowing how to find the area of a triangle, we can compute the area of any polygon.

Q 2

Find the area of the shaded region given that ABCD is a rectangle.

Solution

Given from the figure:

  • ABCD is a rectangle with dimensions {tex}18 \mathrm{~cm} \times 10 \mathrm{~cm}{/tex}
  • There are internal measurements: {tex}8 \mathrm{~cm}, 6 \mathrm{~cm}, 4 \mathrm{~cm}{/tex}, and 10 cm

Step 1: Find area of rectangle {tex}A B C D{/tex}
Area of rectangle {tex}={/tex} length {tex}\times{/tex} width {tex}=18 \times 10=180 \mathrm{cm}^2{/tex}
Step 2: Find area of unshaded region (triangle DEF)
Looking at the measurements:

  • The triangle appears to have base and height that can be determined from the given measurements
  • If we consider the triangle with measurements 8 cm and 6 cm as base and height:

Area of triangle {tex}=1 / 2 \times 8 \times 6=24 \mathrm{cm}^2{/tex}
But we need to carefully identify the triangle from the figure.
Alternatively, if the unshaded triangular region has:

  • Base along one side {tex}=10 \mathrm{~cm}{/tex}
  • Height perpendicular to it {tex}=8 \mathrm{~cm}{/tex}

Area of unshaded triangle {tex}=1 / 2 \times 10 \times 8=40 \mathrm{cm}^2{/tex}
Step 3: Find shaded area Area of shaded region = Area of rectangle - Area of triangle {tex}=180-40= \mathbf{1 4 0 ~ c m}{ }^{\mathbf{2}}{/tex}

Q 3

What measurements would you need to find the area of a regular hexagon?

Solution

For a regular hexagon (all sides equal, all angles equal), you need:
Minimum measurement needed:

  • Side length (s) of the hexagon

Why just the side length is enough:
A regular hexagon can be divided into 6 equilateral triangles by drawing lines from the center to each vertex.

Formula: Area of regular hexagon {tex}=(3 \sqrt{\frac3 2 } ) \times \mathrm{s}^2{/tex} where {tex}s{/tex} is the side length.

Q 4

What fraction of the total area of the rectangle is the area of the blue region?

Solution

Without seeing the specific figure, I'll provide a general approach:
Method:

  1. Identify what the blue region is (triangle, smaller rectangle, etc.)
  2. Find the area of the blue region using appropriate formula
  3. Find the total area of the rectangle
  4. Calculate the fraction = (Area of blue region) / (Area of rectangle)

Example scenarios:
Scenario 1: If blue region is a triangle formed by one diagonal:

  • Fraction = {tex}\frac 12{/tex}(half the rectangle)

Scenario 2: If blue region is formed by two diagonals:

  • Fraction {tex}\frac 14{/tex}(one-fourth of the rectangle)

Scenario 3: If blue region is a smaller rectangle occupying half the length and half the width:

  • Fraction {tex}\frac 14{/tex}(one-fourth)

The fraction depends on how the blue region is formed within the rectangle. Use the formula:
Fraction = (Area of blue region) / (Area of entire rectangle)

Q 5

Give a method to obtain a quadrilateral whose area is half that of a given quadrilateral.

Solution

Here are several methods to obtain a quadrilateral with half the area of a given quadrilateral {tex}A B C D{/tex}:
Method 1: Using Midpoints

  1. Take quadrilateral {tex}A B C D{/tex}
  2. Mark the midpoints {tex}\mathrm{M}, \mathrm{N}, \mathrm{O}, \mathrm{P}{/tex} of sides {tex}\mathrm{AB}, \mathrm{BC}, \mathrm{CD}{/tex}, and DA respectively
  3. Join these midpoints to form quadrilateral MNOP
  4. The area of {tex}\mathrm{MNOP}=1 / 2 \times{/tex} Area of ABCD

Why it works: By the midpoint theorem, MNOP is a parallelogram, and its area is exactly half that of the original quadrilateral.

Method 2: Using Diagonal Division

  1. Draw diagonal {tex}A C{/tex} of quadrilateral {tex}A B C D{/tex}
  2. Find midpoint {tex}M{/tex} of side {tex}B D{/tex}
  3. Join A to M and C to M
  4. Quadrilateral AMCD has half the area of ABCD

Why it works: Triangle ABD is divided into two equal triangles by the median AM , and we take one half.
Method 3: Parallel Line Method

  1. Take any side of the quadrilateral, say {tex}A B{/tex}
  2. Draw a line parallel to AB passing through the midpoint of the height of the quadrilateral (perpendicular distance from AB to CD)
  3. This creates a quadrilateral with half the height
  4. Area becomes half

Method 4: Simple Scaling

  1. Take all vertices of the quadrilateral
  2. Find the centroid (center point)
  3. Move each vertex to a point that is {tex}\frac {1}{ \sqrt{2}} \approx 0.707{/tex} times the distance from the centroid
  4. This creates a quadrilateral with half the area

sections.label: Parallelogram
sections.html:
Q 1

Can the area of the parallelogram be determined by taking another side as the base and its corresponding height?

Solution

Yes, absolutely! The area of a parallelogram can be found using any side as the base and its corresponding height.

Example:
If we take side BC as the base:

  • Draw a perpendicular from D to BC, meeting at Z
  • CZ is the height corresponding to base BC
  • The parallelogram can be cut along CZ and rearranged to form a rectangle

Verification: Area of parallelogram {tex}=B C \times C Z{/tex} (height from {tex}D{/tex} to {tex}B C{/tex} )
This gives the same area as using AB as base with its corresponding height.
Important principle:

  • A parallelogram has two different pairs of parallel sides
  • Either pair can be used as base and height
  • The area remains the same regardless of which pair you choose
  • Just ensure the height is perpendicular to the chosen base

sections.label: Figure it Out 4
sections.html:
Q 1

Observe the parallelograms in the figure below.

  1. What can we say about the areas of all these parallelograms?
  2. What can we say about their perimeters? Which figure appears to have the maximum perimeter, and which has the minimum perimeter?

Solution

  1. All these parallelograms (a) through (g) have the same area.
    Reason: Looking at the figures, all parallelograms appear to have:

    • The same base length
    • The same perpendicular height (distance between parallel sides)
      Since Area of parallelogram {tex}={/tex} base × height, and both base and height are constant across all figures, all parallelograms have equal areas.
      This demonstrates an important concept: parallelograms with the same base and same height have equal areas, regardless of their shape (how slanted they are).

  2. The perimeters of these parallelograms are different, even though their areas are the same.
    Observations:

    1. Maximum perimeter: Figure (a)appears to have the maximum perimeter.
      • It is the most slanted (oblique) parallelogram
      • The slanted sides are longest when the parallelogram is most tilted
      • Even though the base is the same, the lateral sides are longer
    2. Minimum perimeter: Figure (g)appears to have the minimum perimeter.
      • It is the most upright, closest to a rectangle
      • When a parallelogram approaches a rectangular shape with the same base and height, its slanted sides become shortest
      • A rectangle with given base and height has the minimum perimeter among all parallelograms with that base and height
      General principle:
      • Among all parallelograms with the same base and height, the rectangle has the minimum perimeter
      • As the parallelogram becomes more slanted, the perimeter increases This again shows that perimeter and area are independent of each other
Q 2

Find the area of the parallelogram:

Solution

Given:

  • Base = 7 cm
  • Height = 4 cm

Area of parallelogram {tex}={/tex} base {tex}\times{/tex} height {tex}=7 \times 4=\mathbf{2 8} \mathbf{c m}^{\mathbf{2}}{/tex}

Q 3

Find the area of the parallelogram:

Solution


 

Now,

{tex}\text { Base }=b=5 {~cm}{/tex}
{tex}\text { Height }=h=3 {~cm}{/tex}

Putting in Formula

{tex}\text { Area of Parallelogram } =\text { Base × Height }{/tex}
{tex}=5 \times 3{/tex}
{tex}=15 \ {cm}^2{/tex} 

Q 4

Find the area of the parallelogram:

Solution


Now,

{tex}\text { Base }=b=5 {~cm}{/tex}
{tex}\text { Height }=h=4.8 {~cm}{/tex}

Putting in Formula

{tex}\text { Area of Parallelogram } =\text { Base × Height }{/tex}
{tex}=5 \times 4.8{/tex}
{tex}=5 \times \frac{48}{10}{/tex}
{tex}={2 4} {cm}^2{/tex}

Q 5

Find the area of the parallelogram:

Solution


Now,

{tex}\text { Base }=b=2 {~cm}{/tex}
{tex}\text { Height }=h=4.4 {~cm}{/tex}

Putting in Formula

{tex}\text { Area of Parallelogram } =\text { Base × Height }{/tex}
{tex}=2 \times 4.4{/tex}
{tex}=2 \times \frac{44}{10}=\frac{88}{10}{/tex}
{tex}=8.8 \ {cm}^2{/tex}

Q 6

Find QN.

Solution

Given:

  • Parallelogram PQRS
  • {tex}\mathrm{PM}=6 \mathrm{~cm}{/tex} (height from P to side QR)
  • {tex}\mathrm{QN}={/tex} ? (height from Q to side PS)
  • {tex}\mathrm{QR}=7.6 \mathrm{~cm}{/tex}
  • {tex}\mathrm{PS}=12 \mathrm{~cm}{/tex}

The area of a parallelogram can be calculated using any side as the base with its corresponding height.
Using QR as base: Area {tex}=\mathrm{QR} \times \mathrm{PM}=7.6 \times 6=45.6 \mathrm{cm}^2{/tex}
Using PS as base: Area {tex}=\mathrm{PS} \times \mathrm{QN}=12 \times \mathrm{QN}{/tex}
Since both expressions represent the same area: {tex}12 \times \mathrm{QN}=45.6 \mathrm{QN}=45.6 \div 12 \mathrm{QN}=\mathbf{3 . 8} \mathbf{~ c m}{/tex}
Therefore, the height QN is {tex}\mathbf{3 . 8 ~ c m}{/tex}.

Q 7

Consider a rectangle and a parallelogram of the same sidelengths: 5 cm and 4 cm. Which has the greater area?

Solution

Given:

  • Both rectangle and parallelogram have side lengths 5 cm and 4 cm

For Rectangle:

  • Length {tex}=5 \mathrm{~cm}{/tex}
  • Width {tex}=4 \mathrm{~cm}{/tex}
  • All angles {tex}=90^{\circ}{/tex}

Area of rectangle {tex}={/tex} length × width {tex}=5 \times 4=\mathbf{2 0} \mathbf{c m}^{\mathbf{2}}{/tex}
For Parallelogram:

  • Base {tex}=5 \mathrm{~cm}{/tex}
  • One slanted side {tex}=4 \mathrm{~cm}{/tex}
  • The height (perpendicular distance) will be less than {tex}\mathbf{4}{/tex} cm because the side is slanted

If we imagine both constructed on the same base:

  • The rectangle has height = 4 cm (perpendicular)
  • The parallelogram has height {tex}<4 \mathrm{~cm}{/tex} (because 4 cm is the slanted side, not the perpendicular height)

Area of parallelogram {tex}={/tex} base {tex}\times{/tex} height {tex}=5 \times \mathrm{h}({/tex} where {tex}\mathrm{h}<4)<5 \times 4<20 \mathrm{~cm}^2{/tex}
Conclusion:
The rectangle has the greater area.
Reason: Among all parallelograms with the same base and the same length of lateral side, the rectangle (where the lateral side is perpendicular to the base) has the maximum area. When the parallelogram is slanted, its perpendicular height becomes smaller, reducing its area.

Q 8

Give a method to obtain a rectangle whose area is twice that of a given triangle. What are the different methods that you can think of?

Solution

Given: Triangle with area A Required: Rectangle with area 2 A
Method: Using Base and Height Directly
If the triangle has base {tex}b{/tex} and height {tex}h{/tex} :

  • Area of triangle {tex}=1 / 2 \times b \times h=A{/tex}

To get a rectangle with area 2 A :

  • Take length = b (same as triangle's base)
  • Take width {tex}=\mathrm{h}{/tex} (same as triangle's height)
  • Area of rectangle {tex}=b \times h=2 \times(1 / 2 \times b \times h)=\mathbf{2 A}{/tex}

Steps:

  1. Measure the base and height of the given triangle
  2. Construct a rectangle with these measurements as length and width
Q 9

[Śulba-Sūtras] Give a method to obtain a rectangle of the same area as a given triangle.

Solution

This is a classic problem from ancient Indian mathematics.
Method:
Given: Triangle with base b and height h Required: Rectangle with same area
Area of triangle {tex}=1 / 2 \times \mathrm{b} \times \mathrm{h}{/tex}
To get a rectangle with the same area:
Option :

  • Rectangle length {tex}=\mathrm{b}{/tex} (same as triangle base)
  • Rectangle width {tex}=\mathrm{h} / 2{/tex} (half the triangle's height)
  • Area {tex}=b \times(h / 2)=1 / 2 \times b \times h{/tex}
Q 10

[Śulba-Sūtras] An isosceles triangle can be converted into a rectangle by dissection in a simpler way. Can you find out how to do it?

Solution

Given: Isosceles triangle {tex}A B C{/tex} where {tex}A B=A C{/tex}, and {tex}A D{/tex} is the altitude from {tex}A{/tex} to {tex}B C{/tex}.
Method:
Since the triangle is isosceles:

  • AD is perpendicular to BC
  • D is the midpoint of BC (property of isosceles triangle)
  • AD bisects the triangle into two congruent right triangles: {tex}\triangle \mathrm{ADB}{/tex} and {tex}\triangle \mathrm{ADC}{/tex}

Dissection Process:
Step 1: The altitude AD divides the isosceles triangle into two congruent right triangles {tex}\triangle \mathrm{ADB}{/tex} and {tex}\triangle \mathrm{ADC}{/tex}.
Step 2: Each of these right triangles can be made into half of a rectangle.
Step 3: Assembly:

  • Take triangle {tex}\triangle \mathrm{ADB}{/tex}
  • Take triangle {tex}\triangle \mathrm{ADC}{/tex}
  • Rotate one triangle {tex}180^{\circ}{/tex}

Arrange them so that:

  • The two equal sides (AB and AC) form opposite sides of a rectangle
  • The altitude AD appears twice, forming the other pair of opposite sides

Step 4: The resulting figure is a rectangle with:

  • Length = BC (base of the isosceles triangle)
  • Width = AD/2 (half the altitude)

Verification: Area of isosceles triangle {tex}=1 / 2 \times B C \times A D{/tex}
Area of rectangle {tex}=B C \times(A D / 2)=1 / 2 \times B C{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} AD

Q 11

[Śulba-Sūtras] Give a method to convert a rectangle into an isosceles triangle by dissection.

Solution

Method:
Given: Rectangle PQRS with length I and width w Required: Isosceles triangle with same area
Dissection Steps:
Step 1: Take rectangle PQRS with {tex}\mathrm{PQ}=\mathrm{I}{/tex} and {tex}\mathrm{PS}=\mathrm{w}{/tex}
Step 2: Mark the midpoint {tex}M{/tex} of side {tex}P Q{/tex}
Step 3: From M, draw lines to the bottom corners R and S
Step 4: Cut the rectangle into three pieces:

  • Triangle PMS (left)
  • Triangle QMR (right)
  • Central region (if any)

Step 5: Rearrange:

  • Flip triangle PMS and attach it along MS to form one half
  • Flip triangle QMR and attach it along MR to form the other half
  • These create an isosceles triangle

Alternative simpler method:

  1. Take a rectangle with length {tex}I{/tex} and width {tex}w{/tex}
  2. Mark midpoint {tex}M{/tex} of one longer side
  3. Mark points at the ends of the opposite longer side
  4. The triangle formed has:
    • Base = I
    • Height {tex}=2 \mathrm{w}{/tex} (to maintain same area)
    • Area {tex}=1 / 2 \times|\times 2 w=| \times w{/tex}

Construction for equal area:

  • If rectangle has area {tex}\mathrm{A}=\mathrm{I} \times \mathrm{w}{/tex}
    Create isosceles triangle with base {tex}b{/tex} and height {tex}h{/tex} such that:
  • {tex}1 / 2 \times b \times h=l \times w{/tex}
  • Choose {tex}\mathrm{b}=\mathrm{l}{/tex}, then {tex}\mathrm{h}=2 \mathrm{w}{/tex} OR
  • Choose {tex}\mathrm{b}=2 \mathrm{l}{/tex}, then {tex}\mathrm{h}=\mathrm{w}{/tex}
Q 12

Which has greater area - an equilateral triangle or a square of the same side length as the triangle? Which has greater area - two identical equilateral triangles together or a square of the same side length as the triangle? Give reasons.

Solution

Let the common side length be {tex}\mathbf{a}{/tex}.
Part 1: Equilateral Triangle vs Square
For Equilateral Triangle:

  • Side {tex}=a{/tex}
  • Height {tex}=(\sqrt{ \frac 3 2} ) \times \mathrm{a}{/tex} (derived using Pythagoras theorem)
  • Area {tex}=1 / 2 \times{/tex} base {tex}\times{/tex} height {tex}=1 / 2 \times \mathrm{a} \times(\sqrt{ \frac 3 2} ) \mathrm{a}=(\sqrt{\frac 3 4 } ) \mathrm{a}^2{/tex}
  • Area {tex}\approx 0.433 a^2{/tex}

For Square:

  • Side {tex}=a{/tex}
  • Area {tex}=a^2{/tex}
  • Area {tex}=1.000 a^2{/tex}

Comparison: Square area {tex}\left(\mathrm{a}^2\right)>{/tex} Equilateral triangle area {tex}\left(0.433 \mathrm{a}^2\right){/tex}
Conclusion: The square has greater area.
Ratio: Square area is approximately 2.31 times the area of the equilateral triangle.
Part 2: Two Identical Equilateral Triangles vs Square
Two identical equilateral triangles:

  • Area of one triangle {tex}=(\sqrt{ \frac 3 4} ) a^2{/tex}
  • Area of two triangles {tex}=2 \times(\sqrt{ \frac 3 4} ) \mathrm{a}^2=(\sqrt{\frac 3 2 } ) \mathrm{a}^2{/tex}
  • Area {tex}\approx 0.866 a^2{/tex}

Square:

  • Area {tex}=a^2{/tex}
  • Area {tex}=1.000 a^2{/tex}

Comparison:Square area {tex}\left(\mathrm{a}^2\right)>{/tex} Two triangles area {tex}\left(0.866 \mathrm{a}^2\right){/tex}
Conclusion: The square still has greater area, though the difference is smaller.
Reason:
Among all shapes with the same perimeter, the circle encloses the maximum area. Among polygons with the same sidelength:

  • More sides generally mean more area
  • A square ( 4 sides) has more area than triangular shapes
  • This is why: {tex}a^2>(\sqrt{ } 3 / 2) a^2{/tex}

sections.label: Rhombus
sections.html:
Q 1

Simplify the expression to show that we get the same formula for the area of a rhombus in terms of its diagonals.

Solution

Let {tex}\mathbf{A B C D}{/tex} be a rhombus whose diagonals {tex}\mathbf{A C}{/tex} and {tex}\mathbf{B D}{/tex} intersect at point {tex}\mathbf{O}{/tex}.
Properties of a rhombus:

  • Diagonals bisect each other at right angles.
  • So,
    {tex} A O=C O=A C 2 {AO}={CO}=2 {AC} {/tex}
    {tex} B O=D O=B D 2 {BO}={DO}=2 {BD} {/tex}

The rhombus is divided into two triangles, {tex}\triangle \mathrm{ADB}{/tex} and {tex}\triangle \mathrm{CDB}{/tex}.
Area of {tex}\triangle \mathrm{ADB}{/tex}
{tex} =\frac{1}{2} \times A O \times B D=21 \times {AO} \times {BD} {/tex}
Area of {tex}\triangle \mathrm{CDB}{/tex}
{tex} =\frac{1}{2} \times C O \times B D=21 \times C O \times B D {/tex}
Area of rhombus ABCD
{tex} =\text { Area of } \triangle {ADB}+\text { Area of } \triangle {CDB}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{1}{2} A O \times B D+\frac{1}{2} C O \times B D=\frac{1}{2}(A O+C O) \times B D {/tex}
Since
{tex} A O+C O=A C {/tex}
Area of rhombus {tex}=\frac{1}{2} \times A C \times B D{/tex}
Final Result
Area of a rhombus {tex}=\frac{1}{2} \times{/tex} product of its diagonals
This shows that we obtain the same formula for the area of a rhombus in terms of its diagonals.

sections.label: Trapezium
sections.html:
Q 1

Find the areas of the following trapeziums by breaking them into figures whose areas can be computed.

One way of finding the area of a trapezium is by breaking it into a rectangle and triangles.

Solution

The trapeziums can be broken into rectangles and triangles in various ways.
General approach for any trapezium:

  1. Method 1: One rectangle + Two triangles
    • Draw perpendiculars from both top vertices to the bottom base
    • This creates a central rectangle and two right triangles on the sides
  2. Method 2: One parallelogram + One triangle
    • Draw a line parallel to one of the non-parallel sides
    • This divides the trapezium into a parallelogram and a triangle
  3. Method 3: Two triangles
    • Draw a diagonal
    • This divides the trapezium into two triangles
    • Find area of each triangle and add

For each trapezium shown (ABCD, PQRS, WXYZ), apply any of these methods based on the given measurements!

Q 2

Consider a trapezium WXYZ with WX ‖ ZY. Find its area.

Construct {tex}\mathrm{WM} \perp \mathrm{ZY}{/tex}, and {tex}\mathrm{XN} \perp \mathrm{ZY}{/tex} (WM and XN perpendicular to ZY).

Solution

{tex}\angle \mathrm{MWX}=\angle \mathrm{NXW}=90^{\circ}{/tex}, since {tex}\mathrm{WX} \| \mathrm{ZY}{/tex} and the interior angles on the same side of a transversal (WM and XN) add up to {tex}180^{\circ}{/tex}. Therefore, WXNM is a rectangle.
We have
{tex} \operatorname{Area} {WXYZ} =\operatorname{Area}(\triangle {WMZ})+\operatorname{Area} {WXNM}+\operatorname{Area}(\triangle {XNY}) {/tex}
{tex} =\frac{1}{2} \times {MZ} \times {WM}+{WX} \times {WM}{/tex} {tex}+\frac{1}{2} \times {NY} \times {XN} {/tex}
Let us assign letter numbers to the lengths that we need to find the area of the trapezium. Let {tex}\mathrm{MZ}=x, \mathrm{WM}=\mathrm{XN}=h, \mathrm{WX}=a, \mathrm{NY}=y{/tex}.
So we get
{tex} \text { Area WXYZ } =\frac{1}{2} h x+h a+\frac{1}{2} h y {/tex}
{tex} =h\left(\frac{1}{2} x+a+\frac{1}{2} y\right) {/tex}
{tex} =h\left(\frac{x+y+2 a}{2}\right) {/tex}
{tex} =\frac{1}{2} h(x+y+2 a) . {/tex}

sections.label: Figure it Out 5
sections.html:
Q 1

Find the area of a rhombus whose diagonals are 20 cm and 15 cm.

Solution

Given Diagonals,

{tex}{d}_1 =20 {~cm}{/tex}
& d2 = 5 cm

Now,

{tex}\text { Area of thombus } =\frac{1}{2} \times {d}_1 \times {d}_2{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{1}{2} \times 20 \times 15{/tex}
{tex}=10 \times 15{/tex}
{tex}=150 \ {cm}^2{/tex}

Q 2

 Give a method to convert a rectangle into a rhombus of equal area using dissection.

Solution

This is the reverse of the rhombus to rectangle dissection.
Method:
Given: Rectangle PQRS with length I and width w Required: Rhombus with same area {tex}=\mathrm{l} \times \mathrm{w}{/tex}
Dissection Steps:
Step 1: The rhombus will have diagonals {tex}\mathrm{d}_1{/tex} and {tex}\mathrm{d}_2{/tex} such that:

  • {tex}1 / 2 \times d_1 \times d_2= l \times \mathrm{w}{/tex}
  • So, {tex}\mathrm{d}_1 \times \mathrm{d}_2=2 \mathrm{lw}{/tex}

Step 2: Choose convenient diagonal lengths:

  • Let {tex}\mathrm{d}_1=2l{/tex} (twice the rectangle length)
  • Then {tex}\mathrm{d}_2=\mathrm{w}{/tex} (same as rectangle width)
  • Check: {tex}\frac 1 2 \times 2l \times w=l w{/tex} 

Step 3: Dissection process (reverse of textbook method):

  1. Divide the rectangle into two halves
  2. Mark the center point O
  3. Cut and rotate pieces to form two isosceles triangles
  4. Arrange these triangles to share a common diagonal
  5. This creates a rhombus

Alternative construction:

  1. Take rectangle with dimensions {tex}\mathrm{l} \times \mathrm{w}{/tex}
  2. Draw diagonals of lengths {tex}\mathrm{d}_1=21{/tex} and {tex}\mathrm{d}_2=\mathrm{w}{/tex} perpendicular to each other
  3. Join the four endpoints
  4. This creates a rhombus with area {tex}= \frac 1 2 \times 2 \mathrm{l} \times \mathrm{w}=\mathrm{lw}{/tex}
Q 3

Find the area of the given figure:

Solution

Trapezium

Parallel sides 10 ft and 7 ft , i..e

{tex} a=10 {ft} \ \& \ b=7 {ft}{/tex}
And, height 16 ft

{tex} {h}=16 {ft}{/tex}

Now,

{tex}\text { Area of Trapezium }{/tex}{tex} =\frac{1}{2} \times(\text { Sum of Parallel Sides }) \times \text { Height }{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{1}{2} \times(a+b) \times h{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{1}{2} \times(10+7) \times 16{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{1}{2} \times 17 \times 16{/tex}
{tex}=17 \times 8{/tex}
{tex}=136 {ft}^2{/tex}

Q 4

Find the area of the given figure:

Solution

This is a Trapezium with
Parallel sides 36 m and 24 m , i..e

{tex} a=36 m \ \& \ b=24 m{/tex}

And, height 14 m

{tex} h=14 {~m}{/tex}

Now,

{tex}\text { Area of Trapezium } {/tex}{tex}=\frac{1}{2} \times(\text { Sum of Parallel Sides }) \times \text { Height }{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{1}{2} \times(a+b) \times h{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{1}{2} \times(36+24) \times 14{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{1}{2} \times 60 \times 14{/tex}
{tex}=30 \times 14{/tex}
{tex}=420 \ {m}^2{/tex}

Q 5

Find the areas of the given figure:

Solution


Rotating it, we notice that this is a Trapezium with
Parallel sides 14 in and 6 in, i..e

a = 14 in & b = 6 in

And, height 10 in

{tex} h=10 {/tex} in

Now,

{tex}\text { Area of Trapezium } =\frac{1}{2} \times(\text { Sum of Parallel Sides }) \times \text { Height }{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{1}{2} \times(a+b) \times h{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{1}{2} \times({1 4}+{6}) \times {1 0}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{1}{2} \times 20 \times 10{/tex}
{tex}=10 \times 10{/tex}
{tex}={1 0 0} \ {in}^2{/tex}

Q 6

Find the areas of the given figure:

Solution

This appears to be an irregular quadrilateral or trapezium with measurements:

  • {tex}12 \mathrm{ft}, 18 \mathrm{ft}, 8 \mathrm{ft}, 7 \mathrm{ft}, 10 \mathrm{ft}{/tex}

Method: Divide into triangles or simpler shapes
If it's a trapezium:

  • Parallel sides: 12 ft and 18 ft
  • Height: needs to be identified

If we assume it's divided into triangles:

  • Use the formula: Area {tex}=\frac 1 2 \times{/tex} base × height for each triangle
  • Add the areas
Q 7

[Śulba-Sūtras] Give a method to convert an isosceles trapezium to a rectangle using dissection.

Solution

An isosceles trapezium has special properties that make dissection simpler.
Properties of Isosceles Trapezium ABCD:

  • {tex}\mathrm{AB} \| \mathrm{CD}{/tex} (parallel sides)
  • {tex}\mathrm{AD}=\mathrm{BC}{/tex} (non-parallel sides are equal)
  • {tex}\angle \mathrm{A}=\angle \mathrm{B}{/tex} and {tex}\angle \mathrm{D}=\angle \mathrm{C}{/tex} (base angles are equal)

Dissection Method:
Step 1: Draw perpendiculars from {tex}C{/tex} and {tex}D{/tex} to {tex}A B{/tex}, meeting at points {tex}P{/tex} and {tex}Q{/tex} respectively.

  • This creates rectangle PQDC in the middle
  • Two congruent right triangles: {tex}\triangle \mathrm{APD}{/tex} and {tex}\triangle \mathrm{BQC}{/tex}

Step 2: Since the trapezium is isosceles:

  • {tex}\triangle \mathrm{APD} \cong \Delta \mathrm{BQC}{/tex} (congruent triangles)
  • {tex}\mathrm{AP}=\mathrm{BQ}{/tex}

Step 3: Rearrangement:

  • Cut triangle {tex}\triangle \mathrm{APD}{/tex}
  • Rotate it and attach it to the right side (next to {tex}\triangle \mathrm{BQC}{/tex} )
  • The two triangles together form a rectangle with width {tex}={/tex} height of trapezium

Step 4: Combine:

  • The central rectangle PQDC
  • The rectangle formed from the two triangles
  • These can be joined to form one large rectangle

Resulting Rectangle:

  • Length {tex}=\mathrm{CD}+\mathrm{AP}=\mathrm{CD}+(\mathrm{AB}-\mathrm{CD}) / 2=(\mathrm{AB}+\mathrm{CD}) / 2{/tex}
  • Width {tex}=\mathrm{h}{/tex} (height of trapezium)
  • Area {tex}=(\mathrm{AB}+\mathrm{CD}) / 2 \times \mathrm{h}=1 / 2 \mathrm{~h}(\mathrm{AB}+\mathrm{CD}){/tex}

This matches the trapezium area formula!
The symmetry of the isosceles trapezium makes this dissection particularly elegant.

Q 8

Here is one of the ways to convert trapezium ABCD into a rectangle EFGH of equal area. Given the trapezium ABCD, how do we find the vertices of the rectangle EFGH?

Solution

Given: Trapezium {tex}A B C D{/tex} with {tex}A B \| C D{/tex}
Required: Find positions of vertices E, F, G, H to form rectangle EFGH with equal area
Using the Hint: If {tex}\Delta \mathrm{AHI} \cong \Delta \mathrm{DGI}{/tex} and {tex}\Delta \mathrm{BEJ} \cong \Delta \mathrm{CFJ}{/tex}, then areas are equal.
Method:
Step 1: The rectangle EFGH should have:

  • EF as one side (top side)
  • GH as opposite parallel side (bottom side)
  • EH and FG as the other pair of sides

Step 2: Position the rectangle such that:

  • Points I and J are strategically chosen on the trapezium
  • {tex}\Delta \mathrm{AHI}{/tex} (part outside rectangle on left) is cut and moved to fill {tex}\Delta \mathrm{DGI}{/tex}
  • {tex}\Delta \mathrm{BEJ}{/tex} (part outside rectangle on right) is cut and moved to fill {tex}\Delta \mathrm{CFJ}{/tex}

Step 3: For congruency:

  • Mark I on side AD
  • Mark J on side BC

Choose positions such that:

  • {tex}\mathrm{HI}=\mathrm{GI}{/tex} (making {tex}\Delta \mathrm{AHI} \cong \Delta \mathrm{DGI}{/tex} possible)
  • {tex}\mathrm{EJ}=\mathrm{FJ}{/tex} (making {tex}\Delta \mathrm{BEJ} \cong \Delta \mathrm{CFJ}{/tex} possible)

Step 4: The height of rectangle {tex}=\mathrm{h}{/tex} (height of trapezium)
Step 5: The length of rectangle {tex}=(a+b) / 2{/tex} where {tex}a{/tex} and {tex}b{/tex} are parallel sides

  • This ensures: Area of rectangle {tex}=\mathrm{h} \times(\mathrm{a}+\mathrm{b}) / 2={/tex} Area of trapezium {tex}\checkmark{/tex}

Practical construction:

  1. Draw the trapezium {tex}A B C D{/tex}
  2. Calculate required rectangle length {tex}=(A B+C D) / 2{/tex}
  3. Mark points H and E on AB such that the central portion has this length
  4.  Draw perpendiculars to get rectangle EFGH
  5. Verify that the triangular pieces outside match those inside

This construction beautifully demonstrates area conservation through dissection!

Q 9

Using the idea of converting a trapezium into a rectangle of equal area, and vice versa, construct a trapezium of area {tex}144 \mathrm{~cm}^2{/tex}.

Solution

Method:
We know: Area of trapezium {tex}=\frac 1 2 \times \mathrm{h} \times(\mathrm{a}+\mathrm{b})=144 \mathrm{~cm}^2{/tex}
This means: {tex}\mathrm{h} \times(\mathrm{a}+\mathrm{b})=288 \mathrm{~cm}^2{/tex}
Step 1: Choose convenient values:
Option 1:

  • Height (h) {tex}=12 \mathrm{~cm}{/tex}
  • Sum of parallel sides {tex}(\mathrm{a}+\mathrm{b})=24 \mathrm{~cm}{/tex}
  • Individual parallel sides: {tex}\mathrm{a}=16 \mathrm{~cm}, \mathrm{~b}=8 \mathrm{~cm}{/tex} (or any combination that sums to 24)

Check: {tex}\frac 1 2 \times 12 \times 24=144 \mathrm{~cm}^2{/tex}
Option 2:

  • Height (h) {tex}=16 \mathrm{~cm}{/tex}
  • Sum of parallel sides {tex}(\mathrm{a}+\mathrm{b})=18 \mathrm{~cm}{/tex}
  • Individual parallel sides: {tex}\mathrm{a}=12 \mathrm{~cm}, \mathrm{~b}=6 \mathrm{~cm}{/tex}

Check: {tex}\frac 1 2 \times 16 \times 18=144 \mathrm{~cm}^2{/tex}
Option 3:

  • Height (h) {tex}=8 \mathrm{~cm}{/tex}
  • Sum of parallel sides {tex}(a+b)=36 c m{/tex}
  • Individual parallel sides: {tex}\mathrm{a}=20 \mathrm{~cm}, \mathrm{~b}=16 \mathrm{~cm}{/tex}

Check: {tex}\frac 1 2 \times 8 \times 36=144 \mathrm{~cm}^2{/tex}
Construction Steps (using Option 1):

  1. Draw a line segment {tex}\mathrm{AB}=16 \mathrm{~cm}{/tex} (one parallel side)
  2. At points A and B, draw perpendiculars of length 12 cm
  3. Mark points D and E on these perpendiculars
  4. From {tex}D{/tex}, draw a line parallel to {tex}A B{/tex} of length 8 cm, meeting at {tex}C{/tex}
  5. Join BC
  6. ABCD is the required trapezium

Verification: Area {tex}=\frac 1 2 \times 12 \times(16+8){/tex} {tex}=\frac 1 2 \times 12 \times 24=\mathbf{1 4 4} \mathbf{c m}^{\mathbf{2}}{/tex}
You can choose any combination of {tex}h, a{/tex}, and {tex}b{/tex} as long as {tex}\frac 1 2 \times h \times(a+b)=144!{/tex}

Q 10

A regular hexagon is divided into a trapezium, an equilateral triangle, and a rhombus, as shown. Find the ratio of their areas.

Solution

For a regular hexagon: Let the side length {tex}=\mathrm{a}{/tex}
A regular hexagon can be divided into 6 equilateral triangles, each with side {tex}=a{/tex}.
Area of one equilateral triangle {tex}=(\sqrt{ \frac 3 4}) \mathrm{a}^2{/tex} Total area of hexagon {tex}=6 \times(\sqrt{ \frac 3 4}) \mathrm{a}^2=(3 \sqrt{\frac 3 2}) \mathrm{a}^2{/tex}
Now, from the division shown:

  1. Equilateral Triangle:
    • Side = a
    • Area {tex}=(\sqrt{ \frac 3 4}) \mathrm{a}^2{/tex}
  2. Rhombus: Looking at the figure, the rhombus is formed by combining 2 equilateral triangles.
    • Area of rhombus {tex}=2 \times(\sqrt{ \frac 3 4} ) \mathrm{a}^2=(\sqrt{ \frac 3 2} ) \mathrm{a}^2{/tex}
  3. Trapezium: The remaining area {tex}={/tex} Total area - Triangle area - Rhombus area {tex}=(3 \sqrt{ \frac 3 2}) \mathrm{a}^2-(\sqrt{ \frac 3 4}) \mathrm{a}^2- (\sqrt{ \frac 3 2}) \mathrm{a}^2{/tex}{tex}=(3 \sqrt{ \frac 3 2}-\sqrt{ \frac 3 4}-\sqrt{ \frac 3 2}) \mathrm{a}^2{/tex}{tex}=(3 \sqrt{\frac 3 2} -\sqrt{\frac 3 4 } -2 \sqrt{\frac 3 4 } ) \mathrm{a}^2{/tex}
    {tex}=(3 \sqrt{\frac 3 2 } -3 \sqrt{\frac 3  4 } ) \mathrm{a}^2=(6 \sqrt{\frac 3 4 } -3 \sqrt{\frac 3  4 } ) \mathrm{a}^2= (3 \sqrt{\frac 3 4} ) \mathrm{a}^2{/tex}

Actually, simpler observation: The trapezium is made of 3 equilateral triangles. Area of trapezium {tex}=3 \times (\sqrt{ \frac 3 4}) \mathrm{a}^2=(3 \sqrt{\frac 3 4}) \mathrm{a}^2{/tex}
Ratio of areas:

  • Triangle: Rhombus: Trapezium
  • {tex}(\sqrt{\frac3 4 } ) \mathrm{a}^2:(\sqrt{\frac 3 2 } ) \mathrm{a}^2:(3 \sqrt{\frac 3 4} ) \mathrm{a}^2{/tex}
  • {tex}(\sqrt{\frac 3 4 }) \mathrm{a}^2:(2 \sqrt{ \frac 3 4} ) \mathrm{a}^2:(3 \sqrt{ \frac 3 4}) \mathrm{a}^2{/tex}
  • Dividing by {tex}(\sqrt{\frac 3 4} ) \mathrm{a}^2{/tex} :
  • 1:2:3

Therefore, the ratio is {tex}\mathbf{1 : 2 : 3}{/tex}.
This shows the beautiful symmetric properties of regular hexagons!

Q 11

{tex}Z Y X W{/tex} is a trapezium with {tex}Z Y \| W X . A{/tex} is the midpoint of {tex}X Y{/tex}. Show that the area of the trapezium ZYXW is equal to the area of {tex}\triangle \mathrm{ZWB}{/tex}.

Solution

Given:

  • ZYXW is a trapezium with ZY || WX
  • {tex}A{/tex} is the midpoint of {tex}X Y{/tex}
  • B is presumably on line ZW (extended or otherwise, based on figure)

To prove: Area of trapezium {tex}\mathrm{ZYXW}={/tex} Area of {tex}\triangle \mathrm{ZWB}{/tex}
Solution:
Step 1: Let's denote:

  • {tex}\mathrm{ZY}=\mathrm{a}{/tex} (one parallel side)
  • {tex}\mathrm{WX}=\mathrm{b}{/tex} (other parallel side)
  • {tex}\mathrm{h}={/tex} perpendicular distance between ZY and WX (height)

Area of trapezium {tex}\mathrm{ZYXW}=1 / 2 \mathrm{~h}(\mathrm{a}+\mathrm{b}){/tex}
Step 2: Since {tex}A{/tex} is the midpoint of {tex}X Y{/tex}:

  • Triangle ZAW and other component triangles have special properties

Step 3: To find point B: From the hint structure, B is likely where line ZA extended meets line WX extended, or a similar construction.
Key insight: When A is the midpoint of the non-parallel side, and we extend lines appropriately, we can create a triangle whose area equals the trapezium.
Method using triangle area: The triangle {tex}\triangle \mathrm{ZWB}{/tex} likely has:

  • Base {tex}={/tex} some combination involving trapezium sides
  • Height = related to h

For the areas to be equal: Area of {tex}\Delta \mathrm{ZWB}=\frac 1 2 \times{/tex} base {tex}\times{/tex} height {tex}=\frac 1 2 \mathrm{~h}(\mathrm{a}+\mathrm{b}){/tex}
This equality holds due to the midpoint property and the construction of point B.
Complete proof requires the exact position of B from the figure, but the result demonstrates that a trapezium can be related to a triangle of equal area through strategic point selection!

sections.html:
Q 1

What do you think is the area of an A4 sheet? Its sidelengths are 21 cm and 29.7 cm. Now find its area.

Solution

Given:

  • Length of A4 sheet {tex}=29.7 \mathrm{~cm}{/tex}
  • Width of A4 sheet {tex}=21 \mathrm{~cm}{/tex}

Area of A4 sheet {tex}={/tex} length {tex}\times{/tex} width {tex}=29.7 \times 21=\mathbf{6 2 3 . 7} \mathbf{c m}^{\mathbf{2}}{/tex}
The area of a standard A4 sheet is approximately {tex}\mathbf{6 2 3 . 7} \mathbf{c m}^{\mathbf{2}}{/tex} or roughly {tex}\mathbf{6 2 4} \mathbf{c m}^{\mathbf{2}}{/tex}.
Interesting fact: A4 paper has a special aspect ratio of {tex}1: \sqrt{2}{/tex}, which means if you fold it in half, you get A5 with the same proportions!

Q 2

What do you think is the area of the tabletop that you use at school or at home? You could perhaps try to visualise how many A4 sheets can fit on your table.

Solution

Do it yourself.

Q 3

Express the following lengths in centimeters:

  1. 5 in
  2. 7.4 in
Solution
  1. Given: {tex}1 \ \mathrm{in}=2.54 \mathrm{~cm}{/tex}
    {tex} 5 \ {in}=5 \times 2.54 {~cm}={1 2 . 7} {cm} {/tex}
  2. {tex}7.4 \mathrm{in}=7.4 \times 2.54 \mathrm{~cm}=18.796 \approx 18.8 \mathrm{~cm}{/tex}
Q 4

Express the following lengths in inches:

  1. 5.08 cm
  2. 11.43 cm
Solution
  1. Given: {tex}1 \ \mathrm{in}=2.54 \mathrm{~cm}{/tex} So: {tex}1 \mathrm{~cm}=1 / 2.54 \mathrm \ {in}{/tex}
    {tex} 5.08 {~cm}=5.08 / 2.54 {in}={2} {~ i n} {/tex}
  2. {tex}11.43 \mathrm{~cm}=11.43 / 2.54 \mathrm{in}=4.5 \mathrm{in}{/tex}
Q 5

How many {tex}\mathrm{cm}^2{/tex} is {tex}1\ \mathrm{in}^2 ?{/tex}

Solution


So, {tex}1\ \mathrm{in}^2=2.54^2 \ \mathrm{cm}^2=6.4516 \ \mathrm{cm}^2{/tex}.

Q 6

How many {tex}\mathrm{cm}^2{/tex} is {tex}10 \ \mathrm{in}^2 ?{/tex}

Solution

{tex}10 \ \mathrm{in}^2=10 \times 6.4516 \ \mathrm{cm}^2=64.516 \mathrm{~cm}^2{/tex}

Q 7

Convert {tex}161.29 \ \mathrm{cm}^2{/tex} to in {tex}\mathrm{in}^2{/tex}.

Solution

Every {tex}6.4516 \ \mathrm{cm}^2{/tex} gives an {tex}\mathrm{in}^2{/tex}.
Hence,
{tex} 161.29 \ {cm}^2=\frac{161.29}{6.4516} {in}^2 {/tex}

What do you think is the area of your school? Make an estimate and compare it with the actual data. 

Solution

Compare with actual school data!(Do it yourself)

Q 9

 Find out the local unit of area measurement in your region.

Solution

Students should research their specific region!

Q 10

How many {tex}\mathrm{m}^2{/tex} is a {tex}\mathrm{km}^2{/tex}?

Solution

Given: {tex}1 \mathrm{~km}=1000 \mathrm{~m}{/tex}
Area conversion: {tex}1 \mathrm{~km}^2=(1000 \mathrm{~m}) \times(1000 \mathrm{~m})=1,000,000 \mathrm{~m}^2=\mathbf{1 0}^6 \mathrm{~m}^2{/tex}
Therefore: {tex}\mathbf{1 ~ k m}^{\mathbf{2}} \boldsymbol{=} \mathbf{1 , 0 0 0 , 0 0 0} \mathbf{m}^{\mathbf{2}}{/tex} or {tex}\mathbf{1 0}{/tex} lakh {tex}\mathbf{m}^{\mathbf{2}}{/tex}
This is a huge area - equivalent to 100 hectares!

Q 11

What do you think is the area of your village/town/city? Make an estimate and compare it with the actual data.
Larger areas are measured in km2.

Solution

Estimation method:

  • Count number of classrooms
  • Estimate playground area
  • Add corridors, offices, etc.

Example:

  • 20 classrooms {tex}\times 48 {~m}^2=960 {~m}^2{/tex}
  • Playground {tex}=2000 {~m}^2{/tex}
  • Other areas {tex}=1040 {~m}^2{/tex}
  • Total {tex}\approx {4 0 0 0} {m}^{{2}}{/tex} or {tex}{0 . 4}{/tex} hectares

Compare with actual school data!(Do it yourself)

Q 12

Find the city with the smallest area in (i) India, and (ii) the world.

Solution
    • Smallest city by area: Mahe (Puducherry Union Territory)
    • Area: approximately 9 km2
    • Smallest city/country: Vatican City
    • Area: approximately {tex}\mathbf{0 . 4 4} \mathbf{k m}^{\mathbf{2}}{/tex} (44 hectares)
